The Central Question developed by, Steelcase AR+C, in collaboration with ARC’s Executive Team and Senior
Leaders. The Multistudio team will continue to share it as a north star in the 2025 Master Plan engagements.




                                     How might we evolve to inspire a diverse body of Students, to achieve
                                     their goals, provide an elevated service experience, ensure inclusive,
                                     equitable education, support the evolving instructional modality mix,
                                     provide flexibility for learning and working while continuing to build a sense of
                                     belonging for all and enhancing the overall effectiveness of the College?

Space Planning Principles for planning from the “Inside-Out”
The Space Planning Principles use the concepts of Connection, Learning and
Community from the Space Utilization Study to organize space for core learning and
work environments. The principles serve as design guidelines that contain main spatial
ingredients for future solutions. They define the inter-relationship between the different
spaces and combine key settings together into zones.
The plan is organized in three interrelated components:

6        ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Letter from the President
                                                 Facilities Master Plan: Building ARC’s Future
                                                 Together
                                                 For more than 70 years, American River College has been a place where innovation, access,
                                                 and community come together to transform lives. The American River College 2025 Facilities
                                                 Master Plan provides a comprehensive roadmap with a students-first focus that will guide how
                                                 we continue to build, renovate, and modernize our campus over the next decade. This plan
                                                 reflects our shared vision for creating learning and working environments that are flexible,
                                                 sustainable, and centered on student and employee success.

                                                 The Facilities Master Plan represents the collective insight and commitment of our campus
                                                 community, including students, faculty, classified professionals, and administrators, whose input
                                                 helped define the priorities and values that shape our path forward. Through surveys,
                                                 interviews, workshops, and campus discussions, we heard what matters most: spaces that
                                                 inspire innovation in teaching and learning, foster belonging and well-being, and strengthen our
                                                 connections to one another and to the broader community we serve.

                                                 Thanks to the continued generosity and trust of our community, we have been able to bring
                                                 several transformative projects to life over the past decade, including the Diane Bryant STEM
                                                 Innovation Center, the Career Technical Education Building, and the upcoming Natomas
                                                 STEAM Building. These modern facilities expand access to high-quality education, particularly
                                                 in the STEM and career education fields, and prepare students for meaningful careers in a
                                                 rapidly evolving workforce. Each project stands as a testament to our mission of providing
                                                 equitable opportunity and excellence in education for all.

                                                 As responsible stewards of public funding, we take great pride in ensuring that every dollar
                                                 invested supports student learning, strengthens community partnerships, and creates lasting
                                                 opportunities for future generations. Looking ahead, the 2025 Facilities Master Plan offers a
                                                 framework for thoughtful growth and continued investment in spaces that align with the needs of
                                                 our students, employees, and the Sacramento region. The projects outlined in this plan will not
                                                 only enhance our physical campus but also advance the economic and cultural vitality of the
                                                 communities we serve.

                                                 The 2025 Facilities Master Plan is more than a document; it is a commitment to designing a
                                                 campus that inspires, supports, and evolves with our students, employees, and community for
                                                 years to come. It reflects the heart of who we are at American River College: a college
                                                 dedicated to access and excellence, rooted in care, and ready to meet the future with purpose
                                                 and pride. Together, we will continue to build spaces that embody our mission and fulfill our
                                                 promise to empower every student to learn, grow, and belong here.


                                                 In Community,




                                                 Dr. Lisa Cardoza
                                                 President
                                                 American River College

                                                                           that the Natomas Center and SRPSTC provide references for campus cohesion.

9        ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Vision and Mission

ARC MISSION

‘American River College places students first in providing an academically rich, inclusive
environment that inspires critical thinking, learning and achievement, and responsible
participation in the community.

American River College, serving the greater Sacramento region, offers education and
support for students to strengthen basic skills, earn associate degrees and certificates,
transfer to other colleges and universities, and achieve career as well as other academic
and personal goals.’


10 IMPERATIVES from the EDUCATIONAL MASTER PLAN
1. Close the Opportunity Gap
2. Prepare Students for an Ever-Changing Landscape of Work
3. Integrate Essential Skills
4. Tailor Experiences to the Individual
5. Strengthen Connections to Employers
6. Stimulate Exemplary Teaching and Learning
7. Adapt the Physical Campuses
8. Achieve Operational Effectiveness
9. Cultivate Financial Sustainability
10. Bolster Advocacy, Collaboration, and Coordination




10      ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Land Acknowledgment Statement

“We acknowledge the land which we occupy at American River College as the traditional home of
the Nisenan, Maidu, and Miwok tribal nations. These sovereign people have been the caretakers
of this land since time immemorial. Despite centuries of genocide and occupation the Nisenan,
Maidu, and Miwok continue as vibrant and resilient tribes and bands, both Federally recognized and
unrecognized. We take this opportunity to acknowledge the generations that have gone before as
well as the present-day Nisenan, Maidu, and Miwok people.”

exchange of ideals, and helped to shape the collective master planning efforts. Each voice was given the space and time to be heard.

EXISTING
CONDITIONS
EXISTING CONDITIONS
2.1 Overview
The 153 acres site once known as Cameron Ranch has transformed and grown into one of the
10 largest community colleges in the state of California. The vision of the college, “transform
the future of all students and our community through inclusive, equitable education“, is further
reinforced by the commitment and implementation of this 2025 Facilities Master Plan.

2.2 Existing Campus
Aerial Imagery - Google Earth has provided the aerial imagery, most recently dated is from
March 2024. The existing campus hosts over 60+ buildings some of which are temporary
structures scheduled to be relocated or removed. The open spaces across campus are a
woven tapestry that cater to a pedestrian campus environment while accommodating campus
operations carts, maintenance pick-up trucks, bicycles, and the scooters. Most recently the
campus has received new STEM and Tech Ed buildings while upgrading their digital connectivity
with outdoor wi-fi and fiber optics network.

22       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
2.4 Institutional Strategic Plan Highlights


The 2025-2031 Strategic Plan is in the process of being developed. During the 2016-2017
academic year, American River College embarked upon a strategic planning process that
was shaped by three college-wide summits and resulted in a deliberate redesign that is
touching every aspect of the institution including its governance and planning structures.
The outcome included the adoption of revised mission and vision statements as well as a
formally articulated commitment to social justice and equity:




COMMITMENT TO SOCIAL JUSTICE AND EQUITY:

American River College Strives to uphold the dignity
and humanity of every student and employee. We are
committed to equity and social justice through equity-
minded education, trans-formative leadership, and
community engagement. We believe this commitment
is essential to achieving our mission and enhancing out
community.

The Strategic Plan establishes American River College’s
mid-range goals and major strategies. The strategic
goals are the broad outcomes that the institution, as a
whole, hopes to achieve. The strategies are approaches
by which the institution expects to make progress
towards achieving the goals.




23       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
2.5 Facilities Condition Index (FCI)                                                                                                                                                                   

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    




The Facility Condition Index (FCI) provides a theoretical objective indication of a facility’s
overall condition. The FCI is defined as the ratio of the cost of current needs divided by                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            




the current replacement value (CRV) of the facility. The legend chart presents the                                                                                                                                                                       0.00%                           4.43%

industry standard ranges and cut-off points.                                                                                                                                                                                                          16                                12                   22.57%

2.6 Community Forum
Multistudio and American River College
Campus Master Planning Leadership
team conducted a Community Forum
on Campus the 8th of May 2025. The
intentions of the forum were to update
the general community, inclusive of
citizens, students, faculty, classified
professionals, and planning committee
on the master planning progress to
date. The participatory activities at the
forum collected critical and essential
feedback from the members regarding
priorities, current conditions, history,
and development across the campus
over time. The feedback and work has
significantly shaped the 2025 Facilities
Master Plan.

30      ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Space Utilization Study (SUS) - Steelcase AR+C

2.7 Space Utilization Study Summary
Steelcase’s Applied Research Consulting (AR+C) Team
conducted a comprehensive Space Utilization Study (SUS)
post-COVID at American River College with the following goals:
• Explore how ARC Faculty and Classified Professionals
work, model a range of hybrid solutions and determine the
appropriate direction for the future workplace for each group
• Explore classroom usage patterns and the associated
demand, model a range of scenarios and provide input into
current classroom design options
• Utilize the results of this study to update and evolve the 2025
Facilities Master Plan
The Study suggests next steps based on the conclusion that
there is excess space:
“The key next steps for ARC’s Executive Team are to align
on the appropriate direction and scenarios for Classrooms,
Faculty and Classified Professional areas and a point of view
on addressing excess space. Based on these positions the
Facilities Master Plan can be updated and an implementation
approach can be developed.
Typically for projects like this clients utilize a phased approach
to implementing the new strategy which spreads the cost and
change management effort over a number of years.”

The Study suggests additional considerations for
implementation which would require further discussions on
the topic of change management with the ARC Executive Team
and the Applied Research + Consulting team. The approach
described on page 13 entails Pilot Projects and measuring the
outcome over time as part of a Change Management Program.
The SUS is very building-focused and reflects evolved learning
environments that support American River College’s mission
that places students first in providing an academically rich,
inclusive environment that inspires critical thinking, learning and
achievement, and responsible participation in the community.

The following pages are exemplary in providing a summary of
the document that has been embraced and incorporated as
part of the American River College 2025 Facilities Master Plan.

     ARC | Space Utilization Study                                                              Addressing excess space generally has 3 typical alternatives. However, as a public institution located on a dedicated campus
                                                                                                each of these options have their own unique set of opportunities and challenges.


     Executive                                                                                                    Option 1

     Summary                                                                                                      Eliminate Excess Space
                                                                                                                  This option consists of demolishing, selling or
                                                                                                                  transferring ownership of the excess space. While
                                                                                                                  this option is possible, it may be difficult to sell or
                                                                                                                  transfer ownership of space located on a campus.
     Real Estate Savings
     The results of the Space Utilization Study indicate excess space exists at ARC. There                                                                                   Option 2
     are also a broad number of processes and perspectives which are limiting the ability
     to realize additional real estate savings. These are typical across higher education                                                                                    Repurpose Excess Space
     and many corporate sectors. They include:
                                                                                                                                                                             This option consists of adopting alternative uses for
     •    A shift in instructional modality to an approximate equal split between on-ground                                                                                  excess space that is consistent with the College’s
          and online courses has further increased excess classroom capacity                                                                                                 permissions. Some Institutions have considered
                                                                                                                                                                             Co-Working or Innovation Hubs as options.
     •    Use of hierarchical planning methodology for Classified Professionals and Faculty
          tends to result in buildings and spaces being cellular, inflexible and expensive to                                                                                However, repurpose options require sufficient
          adjust                                                                                                                                                             space to be available in a single location as small
                                                                                                                                                                             spaces scattered across the campus can be
     •    Space design is not matched to how people actually work; effective                                                                                                 challenging to repurpose.
          implementation of hybrid programs require an honest assessment of how work is                           Option 3
          currently done and matching the space solution to this reality
                                                                                                                  Eliminate + Repurpose Excess Space
     •    Buildings expanding reactively over time has resulted in a range of constraints
          making it difficult to optimize and repurpose them in the future                                        This option blends the other two options and
                                                                                                                  probably represents the best potential for the
     •    An incremental focus to facilities development and construction has resulted in                         College should there be interest in optimizing the
          new facilities which tend to mirror the historical space solution vs a “bottoms up                      space used.
          approach” which would take into consideration changes in work, instruction and
          related aspects

     •    From a strategic level there appears to be limited measurement and pro-active
          management of space based on utilization; this is not surprising given laser like
          focus on Student success but means space opportunities are not readily realized
          and addressed

Floor           The Community Zone includes offices. The SUS reflects a change in how faculty can work in the
                future to have a higher level of collaboration (“We Space”) while balancing the amount of time they
                need for heads down focused work (“I Space”). This change comes with much higher efficiency
                in the use of space which will make room for more space to be dedicated to Learning Zones and
                Connection Zones.

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         climate. 2.7.7 Experience Concept Zones - Existing Campus
The concepts of spaces defined by Connection, Learning, and Community can be
applied at the campus level to the buildings, open spaces, and circulation areas as
a driver for campus organization. The concept is expanded to further clarify the
outdoor spaces as they relate to the public and students, faculty and classified
professionals. Included in the support of Community Sports are the paid venues                                                    SCIENCE &

2.8 Campus Experience Survey
The Steelcase Applied Research Team conducted a Work Experience Survey to Faculty,
Classified Professionals as one of 15 research methods to inform the Space Utilization
Study. This survey gave the Facility master planning team qualitative data insights to
inform the plan. Key takeaways are on the following pages.

FRAMEWORK
3.1 Framework Overview


“Transform the future of all students and
our community through inclusive, equitable
education.”

			                  —ARC Vision 2020-2031
The vision to develop the campus as a welcoming              The future of teaching and learning at ARC will be built by
learning community is core to who ARC is as an               the education professionals in ARC’s community, and FMP
institution. Supporting students to achieve their goals      projects should provide the tools and spaces they need to
at ARC requires many cooperating elements. Projects          innovate while also sustaining what is efficient and effective in
in the Facilities Master Plan (FMP) should build on          their current practice.
what is working by leveraging the momentum ARC has
already developed, while connecting new initiatives          The 2025 Facilities Master Plan (FMP) represents the
in a meaningful and effective way that allows every          convergence of ARC’s educational vision, institutional goals,
ARC professional to more effectively play their part in      and research-based insights into the evolving nature of
supporting student success.                                  learning and work. Together, these frameworks provide a road-
                                                             map for an adaptable, inclusive, and student-centered campus
Integrating ARC’s work across these areas into the           environment. By aligning physical space with ARC’s values of
design of the new projects presents an opportunity           equity, belonging, and innovation, the plan ensures that every
to have meaningful impacts for students and staff.           investment made in the built environment strengthens the
Research on even small interventions – when they             College’s capacity to transform lives—today and well into the
are targeted to just the right time and place to meet a      future.
student’s needs – has shown that even as little as 30

toward an increase in students. This includes both on-ground and remote.

RECOMMENDATIONS
4.1 Overview

Site Guidelines and Space Planning Principles are provided for future design teams to use
as a foundation before beginning project-specific stakeholder engagement with students,
faculty, and staff. These principles aim to ensure that physical spaces on campus reflect
ARC’s mission, values, and long-term goals.




4.2 Planning Principals

Site Planning from the “Outside-In”                                                           Program Summaries:
The Site Guidelines draw from the concepts of Connection, Learning and Community              Snapshots of academic and support programs not included in the prioritized list but still
from the Space Utilization Study and include Access and Circulation, Vehicular Access,        critical to ARC’s mission. Derived from faculty interviews, these summaries document
Vehicular Circulation, Transit Access, Transit Hub, Bicycle Trail Network and Open Space.     emerging needs and ensure readiness for future development.
A section on Campus Identity details the “big bones” of the 25-year master plan’s outdoor
areas: Entry Plaza, Central Plaza, Promenade and the Main Quad – the heart of the             25-Year Master Plan:
campus.                                                                                       The 25-year plan is a long-range vision for campus growth, including four new buildings
                                                                                              that will complete a cohesive campus composition. Together with smaller open spaces
Space Planning Principles for planning from the “Inside-Out”                                  secondary in hierarchy such as courtyards and small plazas, the massing of these
The Space Planning Principles use the concepts of Connection, Learning and                    strategically placed future buildings will frame the Entry Plaza, Central Plaza, Promenade
Community from the Space Utilization Study to organize space for core learning and            and the Main Quad – the heart of the campus.
work environments. The principles serve as design guidelines that contain main spatial
ingredients for future solutions. They define the inter-relationship between the different    The Promenade is the unifying spine of the plan. Serving as a vital north-south connector
spaces and combine key settings together into zones.                                          through the campus culminating in the future Main Quad, it organizes pedestrian
                                                                                              circulation, improves accessibility, and provides a sequence of spill-out learning and
The plan is organized in three interrelated components:                                       social activity to foster a vibrant student experience along the corridor. As projects are
                                                                                              implemented, each will contribute to the development of the Promenade and adjacent
Prioritized Projects (1–15)                                                                   civic spaces, reinforcing a recognizable identity and cohesive campus experience.
Fifteen near-term projects identified for the next 5–10 years, ranked by Facility Condition
Index (FCI) and program need. These projects modernize outdated facilities, improve           Taken together, the 2025 Master Plan creates a flexible, forward-looking framework.
accessibility, and create outdoor spaces that enhance student life. Each project directly     It addresses urgent modernization needs, positions ARC for long-term growth, and
contributes to the broader campus framework while addressing immediate academic and           reinforces the campus as a vibrant, welcoming environment for learning and community
operational needs.                                                                            connection.

GUIDELINES
4.3 Access & Circulation




Access and circulation shape
how students, faculty, and
visitors experience and move
through campus on a daily basis.
A well-connected and intuitive
circulation system is essential
not only for wayfinding, but also
for reinforcing campus identity,
improving safety, and supporting
inclusive access for all modes of
travel.




83   ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Vehicular Access
4.3.1 Regional
                                                                                                                                                                                               Truckee
                                                                                 Sacramento International
American River College (ARC) benefits from strong             Woodland           Airport
regional connectivity, making it easily accessible to                                                                                     ARC Sacramento Regional
                                                                                                                                         Public Safety Training Center
students, faculty, and staff commuting from across the
                                                                                                             ARC Natomas Center                                                         American River
greater Sacramento area. The campus is strategically                                                                                                                                      College
positioned near major transportation corridors—
including Interstate 5 (I-5) and Interstate 80 (I-
80)—which provide direct connections to Downtown
Sacramento, Sacramento International Airport, UC
Davis, and surrounding residential communities.                                                                                                                                                          Placerville


These routes also link ARC to its sister campuses and
key educational and employment hubs throughout the
region, reinforcing the college’s role as a central and
accessible destination for higher education.
With the majority of students arriving by private vehicle,
the surrounding highway network plays a vital role in
supporting ARC’s educational reach and operational
capacity. The vehicle dependence underscores the
                                                                                                       Downtown Sacramento
importance of maintaining efficient traffic flow, intuitive              Davis
wayfinding, and adequate parking infrastructure on                                                                                                                        Drytown

 85              ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Transit Access
4.3.3 Bus
                                                               Sacramento International
Compared to private vehicle travel, transit access to          Airport
American River College (ARC) and its satellite campuses                                                          ARC Sacramento Regional
                                                                                                                Public Safety Training Center
is generally less direct and more time-consuming.
                                                                                           ARC Natomas Center
The Sacramento Regional Transit (SacRT) Blue Line
provides a valuable light rail connection between the
main campus and key regional destinations, including
Downtown Sacramento and the Sacramento Valley                                                                                                                American River
Station—offering a convenient option for transit-                                                                                                                 College

dependent students and those arriving via Amtrak or
regional bus services.

However, transit connections between campuses or from
outlying communities often involve multiple transfers
or longer wait times, limiting overall convenience and
accessibility.

The main ARC campus is strategically located along the
Sacramento Area Council of Governments’ (SACOG)
designated Madison Medium-Distance High-Capacity                                     Downtown Sacramento
Transit (HCT) Corridor. This corridor is planned for long-
term transit enhancements, including:
                                                                                                                                                 0                  2.5               5 Mile

→     Transit signal priority                                                                                                                                                       Legend
→     Upgraded stations with enhanced amenities                                                                                   Transit Access to/from Major ARC Campus Facilities
→     Potential express or limited-stop bus service                                                                                         Transit Access to/from Major destinations
Although the campus is not directly on the Watt HCT                                                                                                   Medium Distance HCT Corridors
Corridor, it remains in close proximity to this planned full
Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) route—one of the region’s most
significant future transit investments.

Together, the Madison and Watt corridors represent a
broader opportunity to position ARC as a well-connected
transit hub. Leveraging these regional improvements can
help advance equity, sustainability, and mobility goals
outlined in SACOG’s 2024 Regional Transit Network
Plan.

86       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Transit Hub
4.3.4 Buses and Drop-Off




                                                                                                                                             College Oak Dr
Public transit service is concentrated along the perimeter of campus, with key routes
running on Orange Grove Avenue, Myrtle Avenue, College Oak Drive, and Winding                                                   Myrtle Ave
Way. These corridors form a transit-accessible edge, connecting ARC to the broader

4.4 Open Space




The open space network at American River
College plays a central role in shaping the
campus experience. From large gathering lawns
and quiet courtyards, these spaces frame the
daily movement of students, faculty, and staff,
linking key destinations across campus and
providing opportunities for gathering, recreation,
and reflection for both academic life and informal
connection.

91        ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
4.5 Campus Identity


The identity of American River
College—both today and into the
future—is rooted in its history,
commitment to knowledge, spirit
of growth, and strong sense of
compassion and community. As the
2025 Master Plan is implemented,
a more cohesive and intentional
campus identity can be achieved
through thoughtful application of its
Planning Principles.

Key physical and cultural
characteristics—such as historic
elements, open spaces, building
scale, and placement—must be
approached with deliberate care.
Together, these elements shape the
overall character of the campus and
support a unified, enduring sense of
place.



92    ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Entry Plaza
4.5.1 Shade as Signifier
American River College is uniquely positioned to elevate
its transit accessibility through the development of an
On-Campus Transit Center, as indicated in the ARC
Transportation, Access and Parking Master Plan (2009).
Located in Lot E, near the site of the future Davies Hall,
this area presents a significant opportunity to establish
a welcoming and identifiable Transit Gateway and
Entry Plaza that reflects ARC’s commitment to equity,




                                                                                                                                UP
                                                                                                                           UP
sustainability, and multimodal access.

By consolidating bus arrivals at this location and
enhancing the surrounding public realm, the campus
can define a primary transit-oriented entrance—a central
point of arrival for students, staff, and visitors. The vision
includes a shaded plaza with solar panel structures,
offering both climate comfort and renewable energy
generation, while providing a strong visual identity for
those arriving by transit. This environment not only
shelters users from the elements but also reinforces
ARC’s sustainability goals and civic presence.

Strategically positioned, the gateway connects directly
to the main academic spine and unlocks multiple
pedestrian pathways throughout the campus. It re-
imagines Lot E from a functional transit zone into a civic
front door, shaping first impressions and supporting
seamless movement into the core of the college.
Anchored by the future Davies Hall, the entry plaza
becomes both a mobility hub and social commons—
welcoming, legible, and deeply integrated into the ARC
experience.




                                                                 Lycée Français Louis Massignon, Bouskoura, Morocco   University of Toronto Mississauga Campus, Mississauga, Canada
                                                                 (Source: Studio Erick Saillet)                       (Source: Riley Snelling)


93       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Central Plaza
4.5.2 Orientation

A few steps from Gateway Plaza, at the intersection
of the Diane Bryant STEM Innovation Center, Davies
Hall, and the Learning Resources Center (LRC), a new
central plaza is envisioned as the heart of campus. The
upcoming renovation of Davies Hall presents a unique
opportunity to re-imagine the sense of place and arrival
at this important crossroads.




                                                                                                                  UP
                                                                                                             UP
Building on ARC’s existing network of open spaces, the
new plaza introduces a defined spatial anchor, framed
and activated by both current and future buildings.
It promotes indoor-outdoor flow, supports spill-out
learning and social activities, and offers a welcoming
place to meet, gather, and pause—enhancing student life
and informal interaction.

Located along the campus’s primary pedestrian spine,
the plaza serves as a connective hub, linking key
academic and social destinations through a network
of accessible pathways. Its strategic placement and
open configuration enhance visibility and spatial clarity,
reinforcing the identity of the campus core through the
thoughtful integration of landscape, circulation, and
community space.




                                                             Medallia Offices, San Mateo, California   Sacred Heart University Campus, Fairfield, Connecticut
                                                             (Source: Hines)                           (Source: Jane Messinger, Robert Benson, Anton Grassl/ESTO)


94       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Pedestrian Spine

4.5.3 Promenade
The main pedestrian promenade serves as a vital north-south connector
through campus. While this spine exists today, it currently lacks a strong
identity and spatial legibility, limiting its function as a true organizing
element in the campus experience.

Today, the promenade is defined by an abundance of open landscape
and scattered building placement, which weakens its visual coherence
and limits opportunities for activation.




                                                                                                                                          UP
                                                                                                                                     UP
However, there is a significant opportunity to reinforce the spine by
strategically locating future buildings to better frame the corridor,
establish a stronger sense of enclosure, and support a more dynamic
and engaging public realm.

This interventions would enhance the flow between indoor and outdoor
spaces, encourage spill-out learning and social activity, and foster a
more vibrant student experience along the corridor. Strengthening the
spine’s spatial definition would also improve campus navigation, guiding
movement from the main quad in the north to the stadium in the south.
Through these efforts, the promenade can evolve into a recognizable,
legible, and lively backbone of the American River College campus.




View of the existing Pedestrian Spine                                         The Goods Line, Haymarket, Sydney, Australia   CSU Long Beach Peterson Hall, Long Beach, California, United States
                                                                              (Source: Florian Groehn)                       (Source: SWA)


 95              ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Quad

4.5.4 Main Quad
As the northern anchor of the main north-south pedestrian promenade,
there is a compelling opportunity to create a larger, unified Main Quad
by consolidating the existing Entry Plaza, Connecting Plaza, and Student
Quad (naming to be confirmed) into a single, cohesive open space.

Currently, the Welcome and Support Center sits between these areas,
interrupting the visual and functional continuity of what could otherwise
become a true campus green. By integrating these adjacent zones,
American River College can establish a generous, multipurpose central




                                                                                                                                   UP
                                                                                                                              UP
quad—an open space that serves as both a spatial anchor and a unifying
element for the campus core.

The enhanced Quad would support a wide range of uses. Along its edges:
spill-out learning, informal gathering, and everyday social activity. At its
center: passive recreation, concerts, campus celebrations, and large-
scale events such as commencement.

Framed by academic and student-serving buildings, the Main Quad
would offer a strong sense of place—serving as a flexible, welcoming
campus commons that is memorable in both daily life and milestone
moments.




The University of Tokyo, Tokyo, Japan                                   Stanford Campus Center, Stanford, California   Sacred Heart University Campus, Fairfield, Connecticut
(Source: Landscape Design)                                              (Source: SWA)                                  (Source: Jane Messinger, Robert Benson, Anton Grassl/ESTO)


 96              ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Secondary Open Space
4.5.5 Secondary Open Space Network
ARC’s campus includes a network of existing open
spaces that, with intentional design, can be leveraged
and reinforced to create a more cohesive and intelligible
open space system. By strengthening this system’s
identity and hierarchy, the campus can promote clearer
orientation, encourage social activity, and enhance
the student experience across multiple zones. One
key opportunity begins at Gateway Plaza and extends
eastward toward the Ranch House and the Health &




                                                                                                                                                                                                     UP
                                                                                                                                                                                                UP
Education (HeEd) 710–716 buildings. This sequence of
secondary open spaces and internal courtyards offers
a powerful complement to the campus’s larger public
realms. Collectively, these nodes add character and
functional variety, helping form a “village-like” district—a
cluster of buildings and shared greens that evoke
intimacy, connection, and a sense of place.

The existing internal courtyard at HeEd 710–716 already
expresses this intimate identity. When connected
to the Ranch House and framed by future buildings,
the area has the potential to honor the legacy of the
Portable Village while introducing a new generation of
permanent structures designed around shared courts
and smaller-scale gathering spaces. This would create
a district that is both human-scaled and historically
grounded, celebrating the site’s past while embracing
its future. A second opportunity lies just east of the
proposed Main Quad, at the Tech Plaza, the open space
fronting the newly opened Career Technical Education
(CTE) Building. Already beloved by the campus
community, this plaza draws students throughout the
day. Strengthening pedestrian connections to this space
and ensuring new buildings are oriented to frame and
engage with it will help integrate it into the broader
open space network and reinforce its identity as a key
destination on campus.                                         View of the Career Technical Education (CTE) building and Tech. Plaza   View of the Health & Ed (HeEd) 710-716 buildings’ courtyard        North Campus Corporate Campus, Dallas, Texas
                                                                                                                                                                                                          (Source: SWA)


97       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
4.6 Pedestrian Wayfinding
4.6
4.5.6 Connected Campus
Pedestrian way-finding across the ARC campus is
supported by a network of marked pathways, signage,
and open sight-lines that help students, staff, and
visitors navigate between key destinations such as
classrooms, administrative buildings, parking lots, and
common areas. The master plan seeks to improve the
‘mental map’ by enhancing the recognizability of the
north/south Promenade as the predominate organizer.
Necessarily the cross connection east/west paths are
further articulated by increased density of the buildings
and guiding individuals from the main transportation
hub deeper into campus and through to the athletic
department and sports fields, horticulture, and eastern
forest. The cross connection paths solidify a connected
campus.




View of cross connector from STEM Building                     View of the cross connector at Physical Education   View from cross connector toward   View of PE Plaza toward Tech. Ed Plaza   View of cross connector from PE Plaza toward
to the left and former Davies Hall to the right                toward Main Campus                                  Soccer Stadium                                                              STEM Building


 98               ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
4.7 Signage

4.7.1 Directional Clarity
While the current pedestrian way-finding system
provides basic directional assistance, there are several
opportunities for improvement to make navigation
more intuitive, accessible, and engaging for all
campus users. Many existing signs could benefit
from clearer typography, consistent color schemes,
and standardized iconography that aligns with the
college’s visual identity. Interactive digital kiosks
or QR codes linking to mobile maps could provide
real-time navigation support, including information
about building accessibility, construction detours,
and event locations. Enhancing visibility with better
lighting and larger way-finding markers at key decision
points—such as pathway intersections and building
entrances—would reduce confusion and improve
safety, especially after dark.

Finally, incorporating environmental cues such as
distinctive paving patterns, landscape features,
and public art can help create a more intuitive
“mental map” of the campus, making orientation feel
natural and memorable for both new and returning                                                  Campus Zones

pedestrians.

4.8.2 Identified Structures in Need of Replacement
Several buildings across campus have reached their end of useful or serviceable life
use. This is primarily identified by the Fusion Conditions Index. When the index reaches
30% or greater modernizations are recommended. From 60-100% replacement is
recommended. Additional factors unique to each campus may require recommendation
of replacement for certain buildings. Davies Hall is currently in the design phase for it’s
replacement.

103   ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
4.9 Sustainability




Help our people and their environment thrive.




104   ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
SMUD
4.9.1 Agreement
Los Rios Community College District has elevated its sustainability commitment by
establishing a long-term partnership with the Sacramento Municipal Utility District
(SMUD). Under SMUD’s Solar-shares program, more than 28 million kilowatt-hours of
solar energy are allocated annually to District facilities, offsetting a consistent 60-75%
of total campus energy, with all environmental benefits credited directly to Los Rios. This
ensures that a significant portion of the district’s electricity comes from clean, utility-scale
solar generation, advancing progress toward state and District climate action goals.

The Solar-shares agreement provides reliable, cost-effective renewable energy with
predictable pricing, reducing greenhouse gas emissions while supporting operational
resilience across all campuses. Combined with ongoing efforts in energy efficiency,
electrification, EV infrastructure, and water conservation, this clean energy supply
underscores Los Rios’s efforts to creating sustainable, healthy, and future-ready
campuses for students, faculty, and the community.




105      ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Sustainability and Climate Resilience
4.9.2 Introduction
In alignment with the California Community College and Los Rios Community College
District sustainability goals—as well as relevant code requirements and local climate risks,
this section outlines a set of guiding principles to inform long-term planning and project
decision-making. These principles are organized into four key categories:

→     Campus & Site Improvements
→     Building Performance
→     Health & Wellbeing
→     Purchasing & Procurement

Rather than prescribing specific actions or outcomes, these recommendations are
intended to support informed decision-making over time, based on feasibility, available
resources, and project scope. The goal is to help ensure the health, safety, and long-term
resilience of American River College, while reinforcing its mission to place students first
in providing an academically rich, inclusive environment that inspires critical thinking,
learning and achievement, and responsible participation in the community.




                                                                                               Cover Page Image from “Leading the Way on Climate Action”
                                                                                               by California Community Colleges, September 2024.




106      ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Sustainability Goals and Regulations
4.9.3 District Sustainability Goals
The American River Community College District is engaged in climate action with a focus
on reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and promoting environmental stewardship.
These principles are intended to inform decision-making at American River College,
recognizing that implementation will be phased and responsive to resource availability.
District plans are intended to align with the California Community Colleges (CCC) Climate
Action and Sustainability Framework to the extent possible, given fiscal priorities.

When undertaking campus-wide improvements or individual projects, teams should
reference key priorities outlined in the 2024 Los Rios Community College District
Sustainability Plan. Goals include:

→     A completed GHG inventory and established baseline; a Climate Action
      Plan has been released.
→     A 75% reduction in GHG emissions from baseline levels by 2030.
→     Carbon neutrality (100% reduction in GHG emissions) by 2035.

To support these goals, new construction and major renovations are encouraged to
pursue LEED or WELL Gold certification by 2030 where feasible, and existing buildings
should work toward LEED O&M Gold or WELL Gold equivalent standards by 2035, based
on funding and operational priorities. The District may evaluate each project’s potential to
incorporate current LEED and WELL certification standards, such as LEED v5.0.

Pursuing both LEED and WELL certifications, where possible, can maximize performance           Bio-design Institute Building A | Arizona State University
                                                                                               Tempe, AZ
and community benefit, but this will depend ultimately on project scope and resources.
Refer to the LEED v5.0 and WELL 2.0 Crosswalk in the Appendix for comparison.




107      ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Sustainability Goals and Regulations
4.9.4 Code Compliance
All projects must comply with the most current California Energy Code (Title 24, Part
6) and California Green Building Standards Code (CAL Green). The 2025 Energy Code,
effective January 1, 2026, emphasizes electrification, building performance, on-site solar
energy generation (photovoltaics), and battery storage integration.

Projects must also meet the current California Green Building Standards Code (CAL
Green), Part 11, Title 24. A significant update in the 2022 Intervening Code Cycle
introduces embodied carbon limits. Projects over 50,000 square feet must also meet
embodied carbon reduction requirements where applicable, complying with one of the
following pathways:

→     Building Reuse: Reuse at least 45% of an existing structure and exterior; additions
      must be no more than double the reused structure. (Section 5.105.2)
→     Performance Path: Complete a Whole Building Life Cycle Assessment (WBLCA)
      demonstrating 10% lower embodied carbon emissions than a baseline design.
      (Section 5.409.2)
→     Prescriptive Path: Submit Environmental Product Declarations (EPDs) for key
      materials (steel, glass, mineral wool, concrete) that meet or fall below established
      thresholds for global warming potential. (Section 5.409.3)

These requirements align with the USGBC LEED framework, using shared metrics and
approaches to evaluate and reduce embodied carbon.
                                                                                             https://browningday.com/news/lca-stages-matter-when-tracking-embodied-carbon/




4.9.5 Climate Resilience
                                                                                             Reference:
                                                                                                  →   Integrated climate Adaptation
Recognizing regional risks such as extreme heat and wildfire smoke, campus                                 and Resiliency Program,
strategies should aim to enhance resilience over time. Design should prioritize passive                    Extreme Heat and
                                                                                                           Community Resilience
survivability—ensuring buildings that remain safe and functional during infrastructure                     Round 1 Final Program
disruptions such as power outages. Enhancements to support long-term resilience                            Guide
                                                                                                  →   Sacramento Ready
should be integrated as budgets and building cycles allow.                                        →   AIA Resilience Design Toolkit




108      ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Campus and Site Improvement
4.9.6 Mitigating Urban Heat Island Effect
Recommended strategies to reduce urban heat island
effects should be prioritized during planning and
implemented over time. These include:

→     Collaboration with local transit agencies to
      minimize parking needs.
→     Installing solar canopies over parking stalls.
→     Introducing trees and pedestrian pathways
      within parking zones
→     Increasing tree canopy coverage, with a
      goal of 90% native species such as Oaks by
      2030.
→     Coordinating building geometry, entry
      points, circulation paths, and landscape
      design to provide shaded, thermally
      comfortable outdoor spaces.
→     Using cool materials with high solar
      reflectance index (SRI) value in paving and
      roofing.
→     Incorporating permeable paving and
      vegetation to increase previous surfaces,
      reduce thermal mass and enhance
      groundwater recharge.

                                                       Arizona State University Hassayamapa Academic Villages,
                                                       Tempe, Arizona




109     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Campus and Site Improvement
4.9.7 Storm-water and Outdoor Water Use Management
Project designs should incorporate strategies to support low-impact development and
enhance natural hydrology and minimize outdoor water use.

Sustainable Storm-water management strategies include:

→     Optimize Infiltration: Maximize previous areas through vegetated roofs,
      landscaping, and permeable surfaces in walkways and parking lots.
→     Water Reuse: Incorporate cisterns and rainwater harvesting systems to
      capture water for irrigation and other non-potable uses.
→     Bio-retention Systems: Use tree wells, bioswales, bio-retention basins,
      and rain gardens to filter and absorb runoff.

Minimizing Outdoor Water Use design strategies include:

→     Drought-Tolerant Landscaping: Prioritize native, climate-adapted, and low-
      water-use plant species to reduce irrigation needs.
→     Efficient Irrigation Systems: Implement drip or subsurface irrigation with
      smart controllers to optimize water use and reduce waste.
→     Micro-climate Enhancements: Incorporate shading and wind protection to
                                                                                        750 Second Street Residence,
      improve plant health and landscape resilience.                                    San Francisco, California



All projects should reference the Sacramento Region Storm-water Quality Design Manual
to support watershed protection and regional compliance.




110     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Building Performance
4.9.8 Energy Efficiency
To support campus energy reduction and decarbonization goals, projects
should integrate energy-efficient strategies where practical and cost-
effective. The project team should verify the Energy Use Intensity (EUI)
reduction target early in the design process based on the campus EUI
baseline. Life cycle cost analysis (LCCA) should inform project decisions,
balancing first costs with long-term operational savings and resilience
benefits.

Passive design strategies based on the region’s hot-dry climate (CEC Climate
Zone 12) should be prioritized in new construction and major renovations. Key
considerations include:

→     Passive Design and Optimize Building Orientation: Since the building
      envelope has a significantly longer lifespan than mechanical systems,
      passive strategies should be prioritized over active ones. Optimize
      building orientation along the north-south axis to minimize solar heat
      gain and maximize daylight. Integrate deep overhangs, vertical fins,
      and exterior shading—especially on east and west façades. Design a
      high-performance envelope using cool roofs, continuous insulation,
      low-SHGC (Solar Heat Gain Coefficient) and low-U-value glazing, and
      thermal mass materials to stabilize indoor temperatures. Incorporate
      natural ventilation and night flushing where feasible. The building’s
      geometry and envelope should aim to provide passive survivability
      during climate-related emergencies, such as power outages during
      extreme heat events.
→     Daylighting and Lighting Efficiency: Balance daylight access, views,        Manzanita Square Student Housing, LEED Gold | San Francisco State University
                                                                                  San Francisco, CA
      and visual comfort while maintaining an optimal window-to-wall ratio
      (WWR) of 20–40% (North: 30–40%, South: 20–30%, East: <20%, West:
      <15%). Use clerestories, light shelves, and skylights to bring in natural
      light without glare or overheating. Specify tunable LED lighting with
      zonal controls, occupancy sensors, and daylight sensors. The lighting
      design should meet or exceed the California Building Energy Efficiency
      Standards for Lighting Power Density (LPD).



111     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Building Performance
Energy Efficiency (Continue)
→     High-Efficiency HVAC and Heat Recovery: All-electric HVAC systems
      should be prioritized, particularly high-efficiency heat pumps (e.g.,
      VRF, air-source). Consider using Dedicated Outdoor Air Systems
      (DOAS) with energy recovery ventilators (ERVs) to deliver ener-
      gy-efficient ventilation and conditioning. Ceiling fans and individual
      thermal controls in open office areas can enhance comfort and
      reduce cooling loads.
→     Plug Load Management, Smart Controls, and Commissioning:
      Depending on the building type and program, plug loads can
      represent a significant portion of total energy use. Engage early
      with facilities staff and end users to address energy-efficient
      equipment use and behavior. Incorporate submetering and
      advanced Building Management Systems (BMS) with real-time
      energy monitoring to track and optimize energy use. Commission
      all systems in accordance with CAL Green Tier 1 or 2 requirements.
      Implement smart scheduling to manage peak demand and align with
      Sacramento Municipal Utility District’s time-of-use rates.
→     Solar Readiness and On site Generation: Evaluate the building’s
      solar and battery storage requirements in accordance with the latest
      California Energy Code. Maximize rooftop PV and consider solar
      carports where feasible. If pursuing a performance pathway that          Manzanita Square Student Housing, LEED Gold | San Francisco State University
      defers PV/battery installation, ensure pre-wiring for future systems     San Francisco, CA

      is included in the scope. Establish early budget allocations to
      support solar readiness or full system installation.
→     Climate Resiliency: Building and landscape hardening strategies,
      along with indoor air quality sensors, should be considered to
      enhance wildfire resilience, as budget allows. Consider exceeding
      code-minimum solar and battery storage capacity to support critical
      systems—such as filtered air, lighting, and communications—during
      power outages.




112     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Building Performance
4.9.9 Indoor Water Efficiency
Encouraging the use of life cycle cost analysis (LCCA)
will help guide investment decisions by balancing
upfront costs with long-term operational savings. Water
conservation should be integrated in renovation and new
construction projects, guided by the following:

→     Installing high-efficiency, Water Sense-labeled
      fixtures with auto-shut off or sensor based flush
      valves.
→     Using efficient controls, monitoring systems, leak
      detection system, and submetering.
→     Exploring alternative water sources, such as
      rainwater harvesting or greywater reuse.




                                                           Low Flow Fixtures. Boys and Girls Clubhouse, St Helena and Calitoga.


113     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Health and Wellbeing
4.9.10 Indoor Environmental Quality
Design strategies should support indoor environmental quality and occupant
wellbeing, implemented progressively based on project scope and resources.
Engaging students, faculty, and the community in sustainable building design
transforms the campus into a living laboratory for climate resilience and wellbeing.
Some best practices can be integrated in the following approaches to design:

→     Wellbeing & Culture
      Design spaces and programs that support physical and emotional health—
      through access to nature, optimizing daylight and views, specifying healthy
      materials, integrating indoor air monitoring system and dashboards, and a
      campus culture of climate optimism and agency.
→     Stakeholder and Community Engagement
      Integrate sustainability in design workshops that speak about climate justice,
      workforce training, and resilience—empowering participants with skills in
      energy efficiency, wildfire preparedness, and sustainable design.
                                                                                       Missouri Innovation Campus,
→     Curriculum Integration                                                           Kansas City, MO

      Explore with faculty if building systems and data as teaching tools across
      disciplines, preparing students for green careers in energy, infrastructure,
      and environmental tech while reducing climate anxiety.

Engaging students, faculty, and the community to identify sustainable building
features provides a unique opportunity to demonstrate the college’s commitment
to climate resiliency and wellbeing.




                                                                                       Diane Bryant STEM Innovation Center,
                                                                                       American River College | Sacramento, CA


114      ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Purchasing and Procurement


Sustainable procurement should guide purchasing decisions where
practical, recognizing the impact of material and product selection on
carbon emissions and waste generation. Key recommendations include:

→     Material Transparency and Low-Carbon Sourcing
      Prioritize Red List–free, low-carbon, and responsibly sourced
      materials. Advocate for material transparency by specifying
      products with Environmental Product Declarations (EPDs) and
      Health Product Declarations (HPDs). Support local economies and
      reduce emissions by sourcing regionally where possible.

→     Waste Reduction and Circular Economy
                                                                              Glulam Beams. Michelle Obama School, Richmond, California                        Just Food. Douglas County Food Bank, Lawrence, Kansas.
      Reduce waste by specifying durable, reusable, and repairable
      materials. Favor products that support a circular economy
      through manufacturer take-back programs. Coordinate early with
      contractors to set clear waste diversion goals that meet CAL Green
      Code (Title 24, Part 11).

→     Sustainable Food and Waste Infrastructure
      Encourage sustainable food procurement and integrate food
      services—such as pantries or healthy food trucks—into projects
      where budgets allow. Including infrastructure that supports
      composting, food donation, and overall waste reduction.

Conclusion
The Sustainability and Climate Resilience principles outlined in this plan
intend to serve as guiding strategies to help American River College work
toward long-term environmental and community goals. Implementation
will necessarily occur incrementally, driven by project scopes, available
resources, and evolving regulations. Emphasizing flexibility and fiscal
responsibility will ensure that sustainability investments support student
success and campus resilience over time.

                                                                             Healthy Material Pallette. San Jose State University Spartan Village, San Jose, California

management, irrigation and water conservation, and soil and plant nutrition.                                                                                  CENTER

125      ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
04 Kinesiology & Athletics (Continued)
                                                                                                                                                   DIVISION
IMAGE / AESTHETICS
Although the current scope is limited to modernizations, any new Kinesiology & Athletics
facility should reflect the contemporary architectural language established in post-2021
campus projects. Visual consistency will reinforce ARC’s evolving identity and campus
unity.

FUNDING & PACKAGING STRATEGY
Prioritize instructional spaces such as classrooms, labs, and training rooms for state
funding, while spectator amenities and related facilities may be supported through local
funding. Facility upgrades should include site utilities and consider long-term code and
energy improvements.

LONG-RANGE FACILITIES VISION
• Future replacement of the Athletics building could follow a two-story design similar to
Las Positas College, with Adaptive PE spaces, locker rooms, and offices on the ground
level, and additional offices or an indoor track on the upper level.
• Flexible, multi-purpose instructional and event spaces with movable partitions would
accommodate meetings, events, and instructional use.
• Additional square footage is needed to support Adaptive PE, student study halls, high-
efficiency storage, mud rooms, and expanded locker rooms for 500–600 student-athletes.
• Expansion of the current track to meet USA Track & Field standards would enable
hosting of large-scale events and strengthen ARC’s regional athletics presence.
• Maintaining natural grass fields remains a priority for safety and reduced injuries
compared to artificial turf.

The roof will be replaced, and the CMU block will be re-pointed, meaning the old, cracked                                        SERVICES                                             BOILER

mortar between blocks will be removed and replaced with new mortar. This process                                                                      STEM
                                                                                                                                                                                       HEALTH           HEALTH EDUCATION 1
strengthens the wall, prevents water intrusion, and extends the life of the building. There                              ON                                             DAVIES HALL    EDUCATION 2

is also an opportunity to extend the roof to create shaded outdoor space and to improve                        IS

                                                                                                                       HALL                                      RANCH HOUSE
                                                                                                                                                                                        SCIENCE

OUTDOOR SPACE                                                                                                                                                    OPERATIONS
Extending the roof could provide shaded outdoor use, while upgrades to pedestrian paths
would improve circulation and connectivity with adjacent facilities.

SITE & CIRCULATION CONSIDERATIONS
The Boiler Building requires full access for maintenance and future replacement, so it
must remain independent, with circulation around all sides. It is adjacent to the future PE
Plaza, Paramedics Building, Learning Resource Center (LRC), and the Promenade. The
path north of the Boiler Building, across from the LRC and connecting to the PE Plaza, can
be enhanced as a secondary pedestrian walkway. When the new Davies Hall is built, it
will frame the Entry Plaza on the west side of campus, establishing a direct cross campus
connection to the PE Plaza.

IMAGE / AESTHETICS
Exterior masonry work will maintain the building’s utilitarian character while improving
durability.

FUNDING & PACKAGING STRATEGY
Funding strategies include treating this as an infrastructure project packaged with
academic modernizations. Quantifying Greenhouse Gas and Energy Use Intensity (EUI)            Boiler Building West Facade | American River College
                                                                                              Sacramento, CA
reductions will be important. ARC can pursue utility incentives and applicable federal
direct-pay benefits. Key funding scoring levers include:
- Energy & Greenhouse Gas reduction
- Reliability and Code Compliance
- Bundle with Science and Horticulture to strengthen modernization scoring

                                                                                                                                                                                                           OPERATIONS

PROJECT DESCRIPTION
Renovate Raef Hall’s lecture halls to deliver state-of-the-art, ADA-accessible instructional
spaces. Improvements will prioritize accessibility, acoustics, technology integration,
visibility, and flexible seating to enhance student engagement and instructional
effectiveness.

OUTDOOR SPACE
Transform the Raef Hall Quad into a welcoming, functional environment that supports
studying, dining, and informal gatherings through shade, planting, seating, and lighting.
User feedback indicates the space is currently underutilized and uninviting, presenting a
clear opportunity for improvement.

SITE & CIRCULATION CONSIDERATIONS
Raef Hall is adjacent to the STEM Innovation Center, Student Services, Administration, and
Howard Hall. Enhance pedestrian routes between the Transit Center on the west edge of
campus and the Promenade to reinforce a connected, walkable core.

IMAGE / AESTHETICS                                                                             Raef Hall Main Entry | American River College
                                                                                               Sacramento, CA
Renovations should present a modern, high-tech character harmonious with the broader
campus palette. Interiors should achieve the quality of recent campus projects to ensure
visual continuity and institutional identity.

construction.

Promenade—a contemplative garden recognizing the Nisenan, Maidu, and Miwok
peoples.

pedestrian routes to all entries.

students in their educational and career goals. The main campus houses departments
such as English, Mathematics, Science, Fine and Applied Arts, Business, and Technical
Education, each providing specialized instruction and resources. To further support                  FINE ARTS

student success, ARC’s home bases—Arts, Business, and Communication; Health and                                            OFFICES

                                                                                                     AMPHITHEATER
counselors, and peer mentors who understand their specific academic and career goals.                                    QUAD

BACKGROUND
The division currently delivers approximately 60% of its courses on-line, primarily due to
space constraints. Faculty indicated a preferred balance of 70% on-ground and 30% on-            PROGRAM ADJACENCY
line instruction, with daytime classes prioritized and evening options available for working     There is strong support for consolidating Math and Science programs near the STEM
students.                                                                                        building to create a unified, interdisciplinary STEM complex.
The Math Department continues to face longstanding space limitations, often holding              IMAGE/AESTHETICS
classes in peripheral buildings. As on-campus enrollment grows, the urgency for more             Currently a good example of the future campus standard regarding materials and
permanent, subject-specific classrooms increases.                                                concepts.
NEEDS                                                                                            CIRCULATION
-   Flexible Instructional Spaces: Faculty emphasized the need for dedicated,                    The development of a planned transit hub remains a priority. As a major arrival point for
    adaptable classrooms that can support a variety of instructional formats, including          students, it should be clearly integrated into campus pedestrian and transit circulation
    group collaboration and independent work.                                                    strategies.
-   Collaborative Learning Areas: There is increasing demand for collaborative space,
    and faculty are interested in adapting existing computer labs to better facilitate 		
    teamwork and applied learning.
-   Permanent Classroom Space: Classrooms for both Math and Computer Information
    Science should be relocated to a permanent facility, ideally near the Diane Bryant
    Innovation Center, to help create a centralized and cohesive STEM complex.
-   Improved Outdoor Spaces: Outdoor areas between STEM, Davies Hall, and the 		
    Fine Arts Yard are frequently used for informal learning and student engagement.
    These spaces would benefit from enhancements such as shade structures, seating,
    and tables to improve functionality and comfort.
-   High traffic areas for student life at the Library and STEM building entry area.
