The Language and Communication Division at American River College offers a variety                                    HALL                                        RANCH HOUSE
                                                                                                                                                                                       SCIENCE
of courses in English as a Second Language (ESL) and English. The ESL courses cater
to students at different proficiency levels, from Novice-High to Advanced, providing                                                                           OPERATIONS

instruction in reading, writing, grammar, and speaking. On the other hand, the English
courses focus on academic reading and writing, critical thinking through literature,
and argumentative writing. The division aims to enhance students’ language skills and
communication abilities to support their academic and professional success.
                                                                                                                                                                                 PROGRAM ADJACENCY
The number-one skill identified by employers is the ability to communicate clearly in                                                                                            Proximate to the new Science building
both oral and written form. The number-two skill identified by employers is the ability to                                                                                       and STEM building.
work effectively as a member of a group or team. Communication courses are vital to
developing the communication skills necessary for personal and professional success.                                                                                             OPPORTUNITIES
                                                                                                                                                                                 Primary landmark building for students
Communication courses offer instruction and practice in a variety of communication                                                                                               approaching the campus core from the
settings. The program includes theory and practice in public speaking, group work,                                                                                               parking structure.
and interpersonal relationships. Critical thinking, listening, and communicating across                                                                                          Define the street edge along the new
cultures are also part of the program.                                                                                                                                           south parking lot.

BACKGROUND
This division has several unique instructional and facility needs, particularly as it prepares                                                                                   CIRCULATION
to be housed in the new Davies Hall replacement building.                                                                                                                        Pedestrian circulation needs to be
Approximately 75% of courses in the Language and Communication Division are delivered                                                                                            maintained along the edge of the new
on-ground, with the remaining 25% offered on-line. Most on-ground classes utilize HyFlex                                                                                         building.
(Hybrid-Flexible) classroom formats, in which about half of the students attend in person
and half participate remotely. The division has been a pioneer in implementing and refining
HyFlex classroom spatial typologies, addressing key challenges such as integrating large
monitors, increasing room size for interaction, and providing technological support—
particularly for ESL students.

-         Flexible, Mobile Furniture: Lightweight, movable classroom furniture—including 		           supports gathering and connection.
          mobile whiteboards—is essential to support active learning pedagogies. Instructors
          frequently organize students into pairs or small groups, requiring furnishings that
          can be easily reconfigured to support dynamic instructional approaches.

-         Visual Accessibility for Sign Language: For Sign Language instruction, classroom
          flexibility is critical. Students must be able to form circular seating arrangements to
          maintain line-of-sight for signing. Visual clarity is as essential as auditory clarity in
          these environments.

CAMPUS-WIDE VISION
Although the Business Division is not seeking additional space, their insights support
broader master planning goals. Ideas shared during the discussion include:
-     24-Hour Study Space: Create a secure, welcoming study center—ideally located in
the Student Center—that remains open 24 hours and includes access to food, enhancing
student support and campus life.

150      ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Natomas Center
                                                                                                                                    DIVISION
BACKGROUND
The Natomas Center is one of American River College’s satellite campuses, located at
2421 Del Paso Road in Sacramento, CA 95835. It serves as a key site where ARC offers
academic programs and student services. The Center provides essential resources and
facilities designed to support students in achieving their educational goals, while also
contributing to the college’s broader mission of accessibility and community engagement.

NEEDS
-     Transportation between the main ARC campus and the Natomas Center should
      be provided as a college-supported service to better connect the two sites and
      enhance student access.

-     The Los Rios Community College District continues to explore ways to strengthen
      the connection between Natomas and the main ARC campus, ensuring students feel
      equally supported regardless of location.

-     Adjacent areas to the Natomas Center may offer potential for future campus 		
      expansion.

-     Additional on-campus food services are needed to encourage students to spend
      more time on site and fully engage in campus life.


PROGRAM ADJACENCY
Separate from main campus

OPPORTUNITIES
Expansion to adjacent
LRCCD owned open land
and potential to acquire
more

CIRCULATION
Enhance transportation
between main campus
                                                                                           Natomas Center| American River College
                                                                                           Sacramento, CA



151      ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
Sacramento Regional Public Safety Training Center (RPSTC)
                                                                                                                                                                       DIVISION
BACKGROUND
The Regional Public Safety Training Center (RPSTC), is located at the McClellan Center,
5201 Arnold Ave, McClellan Park, CA 95652. The facility is operated by American River
College, is a purpose-built environment designed to support rigorous training and
education for public safety professionals. The campus is organized to accommodate both
entry-level and advanced in-service instruction, with spaces tailored to the operational
needs of individuals as well as public and private agencies.

Each instructional area is planned to uphold the academic standards of the Los Rios
Community College District, with facilities structured to support curricula certified
by the California Commission on Peace Officers Standards and Training (POST) and
the California State Fire Marshal’s Office. The center’s layout and program support an
intensive, short-term training model, enabling high-impact, concentrated learning.

The architecture reinforces the center’s mission by providing functional, adaptable
training environments that prepare graduates from the core programs for careers in
county and city law enforcement, including police, corrections, and probation services.

NEEDS
-     The Center has recently received the green light to proceed with much needed 		
      renovations for mechanical upgrades and leak mitigation.                             Sacramento Regional Public Safety Training Center| American River College
                                                                                           McClellan Park, CA

-     The Los Rios Community College District continues to explore ways to strengthen
      the connection between RPSTC and the main ARC campus, ensuring students feel
      equally supported regardless of location.

PROGRAM ADJACENCY
Separate from the main campus at the McClellan Center

OPPORTUNITIES
The building has the capability to be expanded

CIRCULATION
Enhance transportation between main campus

building will support modern, safe, and adaptable science instruction aligned with 21st-
century standards.

central Science Courtyard. Together, the buildings create a science-focused precinct with
strong connections to the Promenade, reinforcing identity and academic collaboration.

SPACE
PLANNING
PRINCIPLES
Learning Environments & Faculty Workplace Evolved

Recent events have significantly reshaped instructional modalities and shifted student
perspectives on the role of the American River College (ARC) campus—not only as a place
for learning but also as a hub for community connection. At the same time, interest has
grown among faculty and classified professionals in having more choice and control over
how and where they work.

To explore emerging needs in both instructional and professional spaces, ARC partnered
with the Steelcase Applied Research + Consulting Team to conduct a Space Utilization
Study. This effort aimed to:

-     Explore how ARC Faculty and Classified Professionals work, model a range of hybrid
      solutions and determine the appropriate direction for the future workplace for each
      group
-     Explore classroom usage patterns and the associated demand, model a range of
      scenarios and provide input into current classroom design options.
-     Utilize the results of the study to update and evolve the Facilities Master Plan.

KEY OUTCOMES
One of the key outcomes that were analyzed to inform both the Master Plan’s Side
Planning Guidelines and Space Planning Principles is a Concept Map of Spaces that:

-     Identifies the main spatial ingredients for future solutions
-     Defines the inter-relationship between the different spaces and combines key 		
      settings together into zones
-     Maps the flow of spaces through buildings without consideration of the physical 		
      limitations of the building structure

Learning Environments Continuum from the Steelcase Space Utilization Study
Another key outcome is the development of three hybrid planning scenarios, using
DaviesARC
        Hall| as an Utilization
              Space exampleStudy- each representing a different point along a design 			
continuum. These can be summarized as:


      Scenarios Overview:
-     Scenario 1 “Skip”: A light-touch approach, maintaining more traditional layouts
-     Scenario 2 “Leap”: A balanced approach, integrating flexible learning and working
      zones (used as the baseline scenario for this Master Plan)
-     Scenario 3 “Jump”: A trans-formative scenario, significantly rethinking the 			
      Davies Building
      traditional double-loaded corridor model




       Faculty, Students and Classrooms

Faculty Workplace Continuum from the Steelcase Space Utilization Study
The academic workplace remains largely shaped by outdated facilities planning
principles that haven’t evolved in decades. As a result, many campuses miss
opportunities to support how faculty actually work today. In contrast, contemporary
corporate environments have embraced a broader range of work settings—designed
to accommodate diverse work modes from quiet, heads-down focus to small group
collaboration and larger meetings.     This evolution offers a compelling model for academia:
                          ARC | Space Utilization Study

a more flexible, responsive workplace that empowers faculty with choices about how and
where they work.

02 Faculty Workspace and Learning Environments Optimized

                                                                  LEGEND
Blended Ground Floor Concept: Inspired by a faculty
member’s suggestion-what if future buildings split the            Shared Zone
ground floor between learning spaces and workspaces,              Learning Zone
rather than separating them by floor? This concept
encourages informal connections between students,                 Connection Zone
faculty, and staff—supporting the unstructured,                   Community Zone
meaningful interactions that often occur outside the     VISTAS
classroom.                                                        Core

Steelcase Applied Research Consulting Team’s Work-settings + Attributes

       ARC | Space Utilization Study
The following pages show spatial typologies as a potential
kit of parts for the working and learning settings that will
support different learning and work modalities including
those listed below:

                                                     Connection Zone Settings


      Zone Overview
                                                     This zone comprises the heart of the campus with settings that
                                                     support community, encouraging Faculty, Classified Professionals
                                                     and Students to gather, socialize and collaborate. Worksettings
                                                     incorporated in the Connection Zone include

                                                     • Coffee + Connect
      Connection Zone                                • HomeBase
                                                     • Courtyards

                                                     Design Intent
                                                     The Connection Zone is an ecosystem of settings which support
                                                     the learning goals of the College. While these settings support
                                                     individual and group learning they also support the development of
                                                     relationships, enhancement of the College’s culture and provide a
                                                     venue for collegiate debate and exploration of ideas.




                                                     Key:

                                                     ◉ Connection
                                                     ◉ Learning
                                                     ◉ Community


                                                             Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 55




172     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Connection Zone | Coffee + Connect

      Coffee + Connect is where Students, Faculty and Classified Professionals can come together over food and drinks for connecting, studying, and working with each other. It is an inviting and energizing
      destination. It is a place to work, recharge and connect while offering healthy snack and drink options. It is separated and shielded from areas where focus work happens. It should be designed with a range of
      settings to accommodate individuals and group preferences. If possible, the Coffee + Connect should extend to outdoor courtyards to take advantage of the temperate Northern California weather and views to
      the beautiful campus environment. The space serves as a destination for intentional and chance encounters and promotes wellbeing and rejuvenation.,




                                                                                                                                                                                                          Indicative concept only


                                                                                                                                                              Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 56




173     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Connection Zone | Coffee + Connect

      Space
      •    Locate centrally on the primary path, to act as a ‘collision zone’ where people connect, chat and share ideas
      •    Include a variety of settings that support eating, learning, and working, for individuals and groups of various sizes
      •    Consider informal lounge settings with a comfortable aesthetic where people can come together for work and study related discussion
      •    Use different levels of lighting to enhance the design and experience
      •    Provide access to views of the outdoors and introduce café settings outside, if possible


      Tools + Technology
      •    Consider digital information displays to provide up-to-date information about the College, resources, events and activities
      •    Incorporate technology that allows the space to be used for large presentations / gatherings                                                                                      Indicative concept only

      •    Include Wi-Fi and access to power for personal devices such as laptops, phones, chargers, etc.




      People + Behavior
      •    Encourage Faculty, Classified Professionals and Leaders to use the setting to foster informal connections with Students and peers
      •    Include a coffee and beverage experience to support the community rituals that bring people together
      •    Incorporate Student artwork and cultural events where possible
      •    Offer food and beverage options that appeal to the diversity of the College
      •    Develop protocols to ensure the area is kept clean for all users
                                                                                                                                                                                             Indicative concept only

174       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Connection Zone | HomeBase

      The HomeBase offers a place for Students within a program/community of interest to gather, connect, and provides a sense of belonging. Each space is owned by a Department/field of interest and varies in size
      based on the scale of the program. These spaces offer a comfortable, fun, welcoming environment for Students to study between classes, seek resources and socialize. Within the HomeBase Students have
      access to Student Service Specialists, Peer Mentors, Counselors and Faculty. Ideally these spaces should be flexible to accommodate a range of activities both individual and group (e.g. workshops, events and
      project work). It is equipped with the latest technology to support both personal and shared devices.




                                                                                                                                                                                                        Indicative concept only


                                                                                                                                                            Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 58

      •    Design a comfortable, inviting and inclusive environment
      •    Include a variety of settings to accommodate individuals and small groups for studying and socializing
      •    Consider a kit-of-parts to allow the HomeBase to scale up or down depending on field of interest size and needs
      •    Provide display areas for materials and artifacts related to the HomeBase program
      •    Consider a range of methods to display Student artwork
      •    Include workstations for Classified Professionals supporting the HomeBase program
      •    Provide small coffee and nourishment area for Students

      Tools + Technology

           celebrate achievements and successes
      •    Incorporate technology that allows the space to be used for presentations / gatherings / events
      •    Provide access to laptops, desktop computers and printer services

      •    Create equitable experiences across the HomeBase locations

      •    Establish protocols for how the space is to be used

176       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Connection Zone | Courtyards

      Courtyards are an outdoor element of the Community Zone which leverage ARC’s beautiful campus and the temperate Northern California climate. They are conveniently located both within and next to most of
      the College buildings or along major circulation routes and offer appropriate views into buildings where possible. These spaces are active, energizing, inviting and serve as destinations for Students, Faculty and
      Classified Professionals to socialize, study and work outdoors. The vibrancy of these outdoor settings offer the opportunity to build community by creating awareness of Student Programs and hosting events.
      Courtyards also allow users to rejuvenate and connect to nature, enhancing wellbeing.




                                                                                                                                                                                                            Indicative concept only


                                                                                                                                                                Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 60

      •    Enhance existing Courtyards with a range of comfortable settings to support individual and small group activities
      •    Provide shading elements such as canopies, umbrellas, screens and planters
      •    Incorporate Student artwork where appropriate and the ability to hang banners
      •    Design the selections of finishes and aesthetics to complement the surrounding area and withstand the elements




      Tools + Technology
      •    Provide access to Wi-Fi and exterior-rated power
      •    Consider security lighting for safety during evening classes and events                                                                                         Indicative concept only




      People + Behavior

178       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study
                                                     Learning Zone Settings


      Zone Overview
                                                     This zone supports formal and informal learning, wherever learning
                                                     happens. Worksettings incorporated in the Learning Zone include:

                                                     • Classrooms
                                                     • Student Learning Commons

      Learning Zone                                  Design Intent
                                                     The Learning Zone is an ecosystem of settings that support the
                                                     core function of the College which is Student learning and success.

                                                     Classroom settings are reconceptualized to offer an enhanced
                                                     Student and Instructor experience. At the same time these
                                                     Classrooms offer greater flexibility in how the courses are
                                                     conducted and how Students interact with the Instructor and each
                                                     other.

                                                     The intent is to improve the Classroom technology to reduce the
                                                     burden on Faculty. Simultaneously Students, are ensured of an
                                                     equitable visual and sound related experience whether on-ground
                                                     or in person.

                                                     The Student Learning Commons concept is introduced to provide
                                                     Students with places to connect and work before or after attending
                                                     a class. The Commons should include both group and individual
                                                     settings. The Group settings will create an inviting atmosphere for
                                                     studying and informal learning in between classes, while the Focus
                                                     settings will support individual study or online classes while
                                                     Students are on Campus.


                                                     Key:

                                                     ◉ Connection
                                                     ◉ Learning
                                                     ◉ Community


                                                              Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 62




179     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Learning Zone | Classrooms

      Classrooms are designed to support the current and evolving instructional modalities and methodologies. This flexibility allows various methods of teaching and learning to be implemented while supporting the
      unique requirements of the courses being taught. The typical classrooms can flex between traditional lecture-mode, to group-mode, to discussion-mode and back again. The improved Zoom/HyFlex technology
      allows the learning experience to be equitable for both in-person and virtual participants. Technology and tools are integrated in smart ways to make it easy and intuitive for everyone to use.




                                                                                                                                                                                                         Indicative concept only


                                                                                                                                                             Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 63




180     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Learning Zone | Classrooms

      Space
      •    Provide easily reconfigurable furniture that supports Faculty preferences for teaching
      •    Ensure each Student has adequate worksurface space for writing materials and storage for personal belongings
      •    Provide access to natural light and views to the outdoors where possible
      •    Utilize finishes and materials that create an energizing and inspiring environment

                                                                                                                                                                                             Indicative concept only


      Tools + Technology
      •    Provide intuitive technology for Faculty to connect organizational and personal devices to display digital content
      •    Incorporate appropriate technology to ensure all classroom participants, both in-person and virtual, can hear and see all materials
           being presented and discussed
      •    Provide multiple cameras to give virtual participants an accurate context of the classroom to remain engaged in discussions
      •    Utilize vertical surfaces to allow Faculty and Students to display content, both analog and digital (e.g., whiteboards, monitors)
      •    Include access to power and Wi-Fi for personal devices such as laptops, phones, chargers, etc.                                                                                    Indicative concept only




      People + Behavior
      •    Provide training for Faculty to maximize the use of the technology and the flexibility of the classroom options
      •    Include access to technology support for troubleshooting and assistance if required
      •    Establish and display protocols that outline how to restore the classroom for the next class

                                                                                                                                                                                             Indicative concept only


                                                                                                                                                 Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 64




181       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Learning Zone | Student Learning Commons

      Located near Classrooms, the Student Learning Commons provides a place for Students to touch down before or after class. Enclosed pods offer Students a place to join online classes while remaining on
      Campus. The Commons should allow Students to create, collaborate, and focus in both group and individual settings. The Group settings will create an inviting atmosphere for studying and informal learning in
      between classes, while the Focus settings will support individual study. All spaces within the Student Learning Commons should be available on a first-come, first-serve basis.




                                                                                                                                                                                                         Indicative concept only

      •    Energize the space with views to the outdoors
      •    Incorporate a range of furniture settings to create separation and add interest across the open space

183       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025

                                                     Community Zone Settings


      Zone Overview
                                                     This zone is comprised of a variety of settings that support both
                                                     individual and collaborative work for Faculty and Classified Staff.
                                                     Worksettings incorporated in the Community Zone include:

                                                     •   Front Porch
                                                     •   Department Hub
      Community Zone                                 •
                                                     •
                                                         Meeting Room
                                                         Focus Room/Pod
                                                     •   Shielded Focus
                                                     •   Workstation
                                                     •   Private Office: Single Occupancy
                                                     •   Private Office: Double Occupancy



                                                     Design Intent
                                                     The Community Zone is an ecosystem of worksettings that support
                                                     Faculty and Classified Professionals in the variety of activities they
                                                     undertake in their day-to-day work. All four work modes (Focus,
                                                     Collaboration, Learning, Socializing) are supported, and the
                                                     settings are intended to optimize the effectiveness of each mode.

                                                     The ultimate goal of the Community Zone is to build and enhance
                                                     community within and between department across the campus. The
                                                     goal is for each Community Zone to provide a similar experience.




                                                     Key:

                                                     ◉ Connection
                                                     ◉ Learning
                                                     ◉ Community


                                                              Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 67




184     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Front Porch

      The Front Porch is the initial threshold for welcoming, orienting and accommodating visitors to an Academic or Administrative Department. It forms the first impression of the culture and mission of the Department
      and sets the tone for the experience. Visitors can access up-to-date information, quickly orient themselves to the space and learn about the Department they are visiting. The Front Porch allows Classified
      Professionals or Faculty to greet students and visitors as they inquire about services or academics.




                                                                                                                                                                                                           Indicative concept only


                                                                                                                                                               Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 68




185     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Front Porch

      Space
      •    Create a welcoming atmosphere through the application of finishes, fabrics, furniture and artwork
      •    Incorporate artifacts that illustrate the vision and mission of the department as well as past and present accomplishments
      •    Create display points to share up-to-date and relevant information about the College, the Department, Services and Programs
      •    Design for views into the Department’s interior
      •    Provide a range of seating options for comfortable waiting and quick informal meetings
      •    Integrate various lighting levels to create a warm and friendly atmosphere


      Tools + Technology
      •    Consider different creative formats for communicating relevant messages – digital, analog and /or publications                                                            Indicative concept only

      •    Include access to power and Wi-Fi for personal devices such as laptops, phones, chargers, etc.




      People + Behavior
      •    Encourage Faculty or Classified Professionals to use the Front Porch for small, quick informal meetings when appropriate
      •    Develop a process to keep content fresh, relevant and updated regularly

186       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Department Hub

      The Department Hub is adjacent to the primary individual work areas for Faculty and Classified Professionals and contains a variety of causal individual and group spaces. It is owned by the Department,
      providing a sense of identity, belonging and connection for all team members (Resident, Hybrid and Remote workers). It is flexible and can vary in size based on the scale and needs of the Program / Department.
      The Department Hub supports individual and group work and provides people with the ability to quickly transition to scheduled and spontaneous collaboration or find moments of respite and rejuvenation. It
      incorporates layered levels of privacy creating a perceived separation between individual and group work. It offers a range of storage for group related artifacts and materials as well as a resource center for
      printing and supplies. A coffee station is included to house drinks and store snacks and lunches. The space evokes a relaxed and residential atmosphere to encourage conversations, informality and a shared
      sense of community.




                                                                                                                                                                                                         Indicative concept only


                                                                                                                                                             Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 70




187     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Department Hub

      Space
      •    Create a welcoming and friendly atmosphere through the application of finishes, fabrics, furniture and artwork
      •    Consider a kit-of-parts to allow the setting to scale up or down depending on location, Department/Program size and needs
      •    Use both solid and translucent vertical elements to create varying degrees of privacy
      •    Provide a range of settings and postures to support informal conversations and meetings
      •    Include elements of greenery, access to natural light, and views to the outdoors where possible
      •    Provide a resource center for easy access to storage, printers and various office supplies

      Tools + Technology
      •    Incorporate digital technology where appropriate
                                                                                                                                                                                   Indicative concept only
      •    Include whiteboards for analog display and capturing content
      •    Provide multi-function devices with printing capability
      •    Supplement acoustical privacy with sound-masking as needed
      •    Include access to power and Wi-Fi for personal devices such as laptops, phones, chargers, etc.



      People + Behavior
      •    Create a relaxed atmosphere which draws people in and allows colleagues to connect
      •    Encourage Leaders to work in the Department Hub to model behavior and ensure use
      •    Establish protocols that promote the intended use of the space
      •    Develop a process to ensure the resource center is routinely stocked​ and maintained
      •    Encourage users to keep the area clean & tidy
                                                                                                                                                                                   Indicative concept only

      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Meeting Room

      The Meeting Room is located within the vicinity of the Department area. It is an enclosed bookable room for people to meet and come together. It supports various types of collaborative work such as reviewing
      and evaluating, informing and presenting or generating information. The technology provided supports collaboration that is both face-to-face and virtual and offers an equitable experience for those in the room
      and those participating virtually.




                                                                                                                                                                                                          Indicative concept only


                                                                                                                                                              Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 72




189     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Meeting Room

      Space
      •    Provide views into the room by incorporating transparent and opaque glass
      •    Design appropriately sized meeting rooms to accommodate 3-6 people and 6-8 people
      •    Provide seating for “primary” participants and “secondary” participants, with sightlines to the camera(s) and screen(s) for virtual
           participants
      •    Design the size based on department requirements
      •    Offer a variety of surfaces to display content (ex: digital screens, whiteboards, tack boards, etc.)

      Tools + Technology
      •    Offer a consistent, seamless technology experience for both in-room and virtual participants
      •    Integrate an in-room booking system and information board to automate the room-booking process                                                                                    Indicative concept only

      •    Supplement acoustical privacy with sound-masking as needed to prevent unwanted transfer of conversations to other spaces
      •    Provide whiteboards for display and capture of information
      •    Include access to power and Wi-Fi

      •    Develop protocols that promote the intended use and behaviors

190       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Focus Room/Pod

      The Focus Room is located within the Community Zone and is a small enclosed room for 1-3 people or a fully enclosed booth for one person. It is designed to be multi-purpose in support of individual heads-down
      focus work, small meetings (physical or virtual), Office Hour sessions with Students or private discussions. It is both reservable and available on-demand to provide accessibility to all Faculty and Classified
      Professionals. The technology provided supports face-to-face and virtual connection and the experience is consistent and seamless.




                                                                                                                                                                                                         Indicative concept only


                                                                                                                                                             Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 74




191     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Focus Room/Pod

      Space
      •    Plan size to support 1-3 people
      •    Consider including freestanding Phone Booths/Pods, where appropriate, that provide flexibility and create space division in open
           spaces
      •    Provide alternative settings to support different postures and preferences
      •    Enable penetration of natural light into the space where possible
      •    Mix transparent and opaque glass on Focus Rooms/Pods to balance visibility and privacy
      •    Provide backgrounds with whiteboard, artwork, or brand identification for an enhanced video experience for virtual calls

      Tools + Technology
      •    Supply multiple monitors and docking stations where appropriate                                                                                                                Indicative concept only
      •    Include video technology to allow for virtual collaboration
      •    Consider lighting to enhance user camera appearances; avoid lighting directly overhead
      •    Offer consistent and seamless technology solutions that are easy to connect to
      •    Supplement acoustical privacy with sound-masking as needed
      •    Include Wi-Fi and access to power

      People + Behavior
      •    Develop and communicate protocols that promote the intended use and behaviors
      •    Provide a combination of Focus Rooms/Pods that are both reservable and non-reservable / available on a first-come, first-served

      •    Include methods to signal availability
      •    Provide reservation methods that allow for booking some of the Focus Rooms but prevent long-term block bookings                                                                Indicative concept only

192       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Shielded Focus

      The Shielded Focus area includes a range of settings to conduct heads-down work within the Community Zone. Individuals can find a space to tuck away for deep focus work. Located in close proximity to the
      Focus Rooms and Pods, its fluid boundaries and appropriate protocols support uninterrupted focus work. Faculty and Classified Professionals come here to work alone, amongst peers. There are a variety of
      options in the Shielded Focus area including sheltered individual desks, semi-enclosed settings and fully enclosed Focus Rooms and Pods. Working in this area signals to others that an employee is in focus
      mode and prefers not to be interrupted. The range of individual settings provides users with options to meet their needs and individual preferences for how to focus.




                                                                                                                                                                                                        Indicative concept only


                                                                                                                                                            Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 76




193     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Shielded Focus

      Space
      •    Provide a series of enclosed, semi-enclosed and open sheltered work areas that are quiet, private areas within the Community Zone
      •    Include a variety of settings and postures to address individual preferences
      •    Consider high-back furniture and screens to create visual privacy
      •    Include soft furnishings & baffles to help improve acoustics
      •    Consider finishes and colors that create a relaxed feeling
      •    Locate on perimeter of the space away from major traffic routes
      •    Provide access to daylight and greenery



      Tools + Technology                                                                                                                                                                   Indicative concept only

      •    Supply multiple monitors and docking stations where appropriate
      •    Consider appropriate sound masking to minimize auditory distractions
      •    Include access to power and Wi-Fi for personal devices such as laptops, phones, chargers, etc.




      People + Behavior
      •    Encourage people to work uninterrupted in a secluded and controlled space as needed
      •    Consider “No Phone Zone” protocol to reduce unwanted distractions
      •    Develop protocols that ensure concentration by discouraging external interruptions and collaboration within the zone
      •    Include protocols that discourage individuals from “squatting” in these settings for extended periods of time                                                                   Indicative concept only

194       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Workstation

      The Workstation supports individual work in the Department. There is a combination of assigned Workstations for Residents and unassigned Workstations for Hybrid and Remote workers. The unassigned
      workstations can be scheduled in advance or available on a walk-up-and-use basis. These unassigned Workstations provide Hybrid and Remote Classified Professionals or Adjunct Faculty with choice of where
      to work in the Community Zone. Designing the Workstation with a kit-of-parts will ensure future flexibility and provides the user with a range of choice within the setting. Focus work will happen throughout the
      Community Zone and the spacing and density of individual Workstations should be considered to minimize visual and acoustical distractions




                                                                                                                                                                                                           Indicative concept only

      Space
      •    Develop a kit of parts (including work tools, task lights etc.) to provide flexibility and give users greater choice within the individual
           setting
      •    Provide height adjustable workstations to allow users to shift from seated to standing positions
      •    Reduce the height of panels to provide greater visibility, more open communication and more access to daylight
      •    Include freestanding screening elements to signal the need for privacy and no interruptions
      •    Consider benching workstations as an option for Hybrid and Remote workers
      •    Identify individual and group storage needs both at a workstation and Department level

      Tools + Technology
      •    Offer consistent and seamless technology solutions and tools to effectively support in person and virtual connections                                                                    Indicative concept only

      •    Consider appropriate sound masking to minimize auditory distractions
      •    Include access to power and Wi-Fi for personal devices such as laptops, phones, chargers, etc.

      •    Develop protocols to communicate accepted behaviors at the workstations in the Department Hub
      •    Establish protocols for scheduled video calls that occur in enclosed spaces to minimize distractions in the open neighborhood

196       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Private Office: Single Occupancy

      The Private Office is intended to support individual work, small meetings, virtual calls with audio and visual needs, and one-on-one confidential conversations. The Private Office is located within the Community
      Zone and enhances Office Hour sessions with Students and interactions with other Faculty and Classified Professionals. The Office may be assigned, unassigned or shared, and may accommodate artifacts of
      one or more staff member depending on the hybrid strategy being implemented to support Faculty and Departmental needs. Designing the Private Office with a kit-of-parts will ensure future flexibility and provides
      the user with a range of choices within the setting. Integrating storage, tools and digital technologies in the Private Office ensures that personal workstyles, collaboration and the creative process are supported.




                                                                                                                                                                                                            Indicative concept only

      Space
      •    Design the Private Office for multi-use by including a collaboration space for an additional one to two people
      •    Develop a kit of parts to provide flexibility and greater choice within the individual setting
      •    Include both transparent and solid boundaries to vary levels of privacy but still allow daylight to extend through the space
      •    Include semi-transparent glass walls or transparent sidelights to provide both visual privacy and views to the exterior
      •    Provide height adjustable desks to allow users to shift from seated to standing positions​
      •    Consider the storage and display needs for Faculty and Classified Professionals: lockable, open shelving for books, credentials etc.

      Tools + Technology
      •    Provide consistent and seamless technology solutions to support in person and virtual connections

      •    Supply dual or curved monitors and docking stations at the desk where appropriate
      •    Include access to power and Wi-Fi for personal devices such as laptops, phones, chargers, etc.




      People + Behavior
      •    Develop protocols that promote the intended use and behaviors, recognizing that work activities and work styles vary by individual and
           department
      •    Create a welcoming environment for Students to interact with Faculty during Office Hours
      •    Establish protocols for signaling occupancy, availability or the need for privacy

                                                                                                                                                                                                Indicative concept only


                                                                                                                                                    Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 81




198       ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Private Office: Double Occupancy

      The Double Occupancy Private Office hosts multiple users and is designed to support the needs of up to two occupants using the office at the same time. Shielding elements within the office allow the two
      occupants to focus on individual work, while relieving concerns of privacy and concentration. Lockable storage keeps each occupant’s belongings safe while others are using the space. ​The Office may be
      assigned, unassigned or shared depending on the hybrid strategy being implemented to support Faculty and Departmental needs. Designing the Office with a kit-of-parts will ensure future flexibility and provides
      the user with a range of choices within the setting. Integrating storage, tools and digital technologies ensures that personal workstyles and collaboration are supported. It is located in the Community Zone in close
      proximity to alternative settings that support collaborative and social activities.




                                                                                                                                                                                                             Indicative concept only


                                                                                                                                                                 Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 82




199     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
      ARC | Space Utilization Study




      Worksettings
      Community Zone | Private Office: Double Occupancy

      Space
      •    Design the office to support all occupants’ needs for work, display and storage
      •    Provide shielding elements to define each occupant’s work area and support the need for focus
      •    Develop a kit of parts to provide flexibility and greater choice within the setting
      •    Include both transparent and solid boundaries to vary levels of privacy but still allow daylight to extend through the space
      •    Include semi-transparent glass walls or transparent sidelights to provide both visual privacy and views to the exterior
      •    Provide height adjustable desks to allow users to shift from seated to standing positions​
      •    Consider the storage and display needs for Faculty and Classified Professionals: lockable, open shelving for books, credentials etc.

      Tools + Technology
                                                                                                                                                                                              Indicative concept only
      •    Provide consistent and seamless technology solutions to support in person and virtual connections
      •    Supply dual or curved monitors and docking stations at the desk
      •    Include access to power and Wi-Fi for personal devices such as laptops, phones, chargers, etc.




      People + Behavior
      •    Develop protocols that promote the intended use and behaviors, recognizing that Faculty work activities and work styles vary by
           individual and department
      •    Create a welcoming environment for Students to interact with Faculty during Office Hours
      •    Establish protocols for signaling occupancy, availability or the need for privacy
                                                                                                                                                                                              Indicative concept only


                                                                                                                                                  Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 83

       Workshop - Community Forum

 Feedback & Key Concerns Raised by Attendees
 Space Needs & Enrollment:                                                                      M*    1-Aug
 -Concern over reducing classroom capacity limiting future enrollment growth.
 -Importance of balancing efficiency with teaching effectiveness.
 Multistudio is to incorporate into final FMP.

 Accessibility Issues:                                                                          M*    1-Aug
 -Elevator shortages in new buildings (insufficient for ADA needs).
 -Request to exceed code minimums proactively (e.g., multiple elevators).
 -Need for more inclusive design for disability services and private student spaces.
 Multistudio is to incorporate into final FMP.
 Sustainability & Climate Action:                                                               M*    1-Aug
 -Request to integrate climate change resilience in building design, not just energy systems.
 -Leverage existing campus assets like Grider Grove for environmental education and
 community engagement.
 Multistudio to schedule follow up meeting with new LRCCD Sustainability Lead


 Next Steps
 Design Guidelines Development (Summer 2025):                                                   M*    9-Sep
 -Will include department-specific needs, flexible design principles, and accessibility
 standards.
 -Stakeholder review sessions planned.
 Continued Community Engagement:                                                                ARC   15-Sep
 -Survey open for further feedback.
 -Feedback stations and Miro boards used for collecting more input.


 Long-Term Vision:                                                                              ARC
 -Aligning with 25-year infrastructure and growth goals.                                              2026
 -Balancing immediate funding opportunities with long-term campus experience
 improvements.

229     ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
230   ARC-Facilities Master Plan | December 2025
American River College Funding Strategies to Support the
Facilities Master Plan (Through 2035)
Updated August 15, 2025
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
American River College (ARC) is updating its Facilities Master Plan (FMP) through 2035. Delivering the plan requires an                     4. Optimize sequencing and timing. Milestones will be synchronized with state submission windows, trustee actions, and
integrated capital strategy that coordinates three primary sources of funding: state participation under Proposition Two,¹ a local             community engagement to reduce schedule risk and escalation. Early enabling packages will be considered where they
general obligation (GO) bond measure targeted for November 2026,² and complementary public financing instruments,                              accelerate begin‑construction evidence or mitigate lead‑time risks for specialized equipment.
particularly Energy Conservation Assistance Act (ECAA) loans.³ ²⁵ Together, these sources are structured to remediate deferred
maintenance, modernize instructional environments, improve energy performance, and harden campus infrastructure while                    Federal update (effective July 4, 2025). H.R. 1 (the One Big Beautiful Bill Act) amended selected Inflation Reduction Act (IRA)
holding the district’s tax rate constant.                                                                                                provisions. Direct pay under Internal Revenue Code Section 6417 remains available to tax‑exempt entities such as ARC, but
                                                                                                                                         schedules are tighter, domestic‑content requirements are stricter, and vendor diligence is required to avoid prohibited‑entity
This report translates policy, finance, and program constraints into an actionable pathway for project execution. It identifies          conflicts.²⁴ ²⁶ ²⁷ ²⁹ ARC should memorialize procedures for domestic‑content verification, begin‑construction documentation, and
priority projects, sequencing logic, funding mechanisms, and governance practices that collectively de‑risk delivery. It also            Foreign Entity of Concern (FEOC) screening within standard procurement templates and owner‑furnished specifications.
addresses the July 4, 2025 federal changes to clean‑energy tax credits and explains how ARC can continue to monetize direct
pay while meeting domestic‑content and vendor‑diligence requirements.
                                                                                                                                         How to Read This Report
Core Funding Principles                                                                                                                     ●   Acronyms Used in This Report
                                                                                                                                            ●   Sections 1–2 orient you to the timeline and strategic context.
   1. Maximize state cost sharing. Project scopes will be packaged to meet Proposition Two eligibility criteria and optimize                ●   Section 3 details condition, capacity, and utilization data used to justify the sequence.
      scoring for life safety, modernization, and energy efficiency so that the state share approaches the allowable ceiling. The           ●   Section 4 provides the updated priority list and compliance table calibrated to CCCCO scoring.
      college will emphasize clearly documented hazards, code compliance, instructional impacts, and measurable operating                   ●   Section 5 gives the project profiles.
      savings to strengthen Final Project Proposal (FPP) scoring.¹⁰                                                                         ●   Sections 6–7 outline funding capacity and the integrated financing strategy.
   2. Protect fiscal resilience. The proposed rate‑extension GO bond will remain within existing levy limits and align with                 ●   Section 8 addresses federal changes to IRA direct pay and the implications for procurement.
      board‑adopted reserve policies, preserving liquidity and credit strength across economic cycles. Reserve draws, if any,               ●   Sections 9–10 summarize risk and provide responses to common concerns.
      will be temporary and backfilled by state reimbursements and energy incentives.²                                                      ●   Appendix A contains the alignment matrix for submittal preparation and board communications.
   3. Maintain schedule continuity. Where timing gaps arise between state authorization and cash disbursement, the district                 ●   REFERENCES (Chicago Notes & Bibliography)
      will deploy zero‑interest ECAA loans to carry design and early works so that critical‑path activities are uninterrupted.³ ²⁵
      The objective is to avoid stop‑start construction patterns that elevate cost and risk.

   ●   Flexible learning. Redesign STEM labs and health‑science simulation spaces to support interdisciplinary                      Section 3. CONDITION AND CAPACITY ANALYSIS
       pedagogy and industry certification. Include adaptable utilities, reconfigurable casework, and standardized
       safety systems to reduce rework across projects.                                                                             This analysis synthesizes Facility Condition Index (FCI) data, detailed space‑use studies, and code‑compliance audits
   ●   Student‑centered services. Create consolidated hubs that streamline advising, enrollment, and wellness. Provide              to prioritize investment by risk, academic impact, and regulatory urgency. Narrative references follow the August 13,
       universal design and co‑location of high‑demand services to reduce student friction and improve completion.                  2025 ranking:
   ●   Sustainability and resilience. Adopt net‑zero‑ready standards, renewable‑energy canopies, and greywater
       systems that reduce operating costs and climate risk. Integrate energy storage where it supports resilience and              (1) Corp Building,
       demand‑charge management.                                                                                                    (2) Science,
   ●   Campus cohesion. Improve outdoor learning and pedestrian connectivity, with clear wayfinding and shaded                      (3) Horticulture
                                                                                                                                    (4) Kinesiology & Athletics
       gathering spaces that support community events and informal learning.
                                                                                                                                    (5) Boiler Building & Central Plant
                                                                                                                                    (6) Stadium
Enrollment and Workforce Trends                                                                                                     (7) West Transit Center
                                                                                                                                    (8) Health & Education (New Paramedics)
While full‑time‑equivalent enrollment has leveled, career pathways in health, information technology, and clean‑energy trades are
expanding at roughly 8 percent per year.⁶ Regional projections indicate more than 9,000 new positions in healthcare support and     FCI Rankings
green construction by 2030. The plan therefore balances large lecture capacity with modular career‑technical labs and simulation
spaces to match labor‑market demand. Program sequencing will give early consideration to allied‑health laboratories,                   ●   Boiler Building & Central Plant: 90.8% FCI. Replacement of thermal‑generation equipment is urgent and should
basic‑needs hubs, and essential infrastructure that removes capacity constraints in high‑demand pathways.                                  be coordinated with academic‑building modernization to maintain eligibility and minimize campus disruptions.
                                                                                                                                       ●   Science Building: 76.7% FCI. Lab systems and envelope failures jeopardize instructional continuity and can be
Planning Principles                                                                                                                        addressed in a single modernization package that includes ventilation, exhaust, accessibility, and flexible‑lab
                                                                                                                                           planning.
   1. Life safety first. Pursuant to the August 13, 2025 priority update, focus near‑term effort on the Corp Building,                 ●   Horticulture Building: 66.4% FCI. Legacy greenhouse systems constrain curriculum delivery and should be
      Science Building, Horticulture Building, and Kinesiology & Athletics. Treat the Boiler Building & Central Plant as                   upgraded alongside classroom and lab modernization to raise utilization.
      infrastructure packaged with academic scopes to maximize eligibility. Package scopes to present clear
                                                                                                                                    Interpretive note. FCI values indicate relative condition; they do not, by themselves, determine priority. Priority is
      life‑safety improvements, accessibility compliance, and hazardous‑material abatement.
                                                                                                                                    derived from a composite of condition, instruction, safety, readiness, and financing.
   2. Workforce alignment. Sequence projects that expand high‑demand programs such as Emergency Medical
      Technology and Certified Nursing Assistant, information technology, and skilled trades. Early delivery increases
      enrollment capacity and creates visible community benefits that support voter confidence.                                     Space Utilization Analysis
   3. Energy and cost efficiency. Apply Proposition Two and ECAA resources to scopes with verifiable operating
      savings and resilience benefits. Document energy measures with third‑party calculators and include                            Utilization remains below targets, indicating scheduling inefficiencies and latent capacity: lecture rooms operate at 47
      commissioning to ensure persistence of savings.                                                                               percent versus a 70 percent target; career‑technical labs at 28 percent versus an 85 percent target.⁶ The college should
   4. Community engagement. Maintain a structured stakeholder program with webinars, tours, and targeted                            standardize scheduling rules, right‑size room counts during modernization, and pursue cross‑listing where course
      surveying to sustain trust and build support ahead of the November 2026 election. Provide transparent                         conflicts suppress utilization.
      dashboards for scope, schedule, budget, and change management once projects move into construction.

Section 6. FUNDING CAPACITY ASSESSMENT
Funding capacity rests on reserves, debt profile, and available levy room under multiple economic scenarios. Even with      Program controls. Governance will include a program management office; monthly dashboards to track budget,
conservative assumptions, Los Rios Community College District (LRCCD) can meet local participation without                  schedule, and risk; and change thresholds that trigger executive review. Independent cost estimating will be used at
increasing the levy. The analysis uses historical AV growth tempered to a conservative case and assumes interest rates      schematic and design‑development milestones to validate budgets before bid.²⁰
that reflect current market conditions.


Unrestricted Reserves                                                                                                       Section 7. INTEGRATED FUNDING STRATEGY
Los Rios reports $61.5 million in unrestricted reserves for FY 2024–25, with a projected increase to $67.9 million by FY    A phased strategy aligns external and local resources to sustain delivery:
2026–27, exceeding the 5 percent policy floor of roughly $33 million.² ⁷ Reserves provide working capital for design and
early works and support credit strength during multi‑year delivery.                                                            1. State share (Proposition Two). Prioritize scopes qualifying for high‑value life‑safety and modernization
                                                                                                                                  categories to approach a 50 percent state share.
Existing Debt Profile                                                                                                          2. Local share (GO bond). Place a November 2026 rate‑extension measure calibrated to cover the local match while
                                                                                                                                  maintaining the current levy.
Annual debt service on existing bonds totals roughly $10 million, or 1.3 percent of the general fund. Within the current       3. Bridge financing. Utilize ECAA loans¹² ²⁵ for schematic design, early works, and pre‑bond procurement to hold
levy, the district can issue up to $150 million in additional GO bonds before reaching an 8 percent debt‑to‑expenditure           schedule.
cap. The debt portfolio will benefit from careful structuring of series and amortization to match project cash flows and       4. Grants and incentives. Monetize IRA direct‑pay credits⁹, as modified by H.R. 1, and pursue state efficiency
state reimbursement timing.                                                                                                       financing to reduce net cost. Emphasize domestic content, credible begin‑construction evidence, and supplier
                                                                                                                                  diligence to avoid elective‑pay reductions or disallowance.²⁶ ²⁷ ²⁸ ²⁹
Tax‑Rate Capacity
                                                                                                                            Cash Flow and Match Management
With 88 percent of assessed value within the School Facilities Improvement District (SFID), the district can place a
rate‑extension bond that covers the local match without increasing the existing $0.025 per $100 levy.¹¹ Rate‑extension         ●   State reimbursement timing. Assume lags between FPP approval and cash receipt. Use ECAA proceeds to
language and public communications will emphasize tax neutrality, visible campus improvements, and accountability.                 maintain design and early‑construction momentum.²⁵
                                                                                                                               ●   Series structuring. Size initial bond series to fund preconstruction and early academic modernization while
                                                                                                                                   preserving capacity for mid‑cycle issuance aligned to FPP approvals.
Sensitivity Analysis                                                                                                           ●   Direct‑pay planning. For eligible energy scopes, plan design and procurement to meet domestic‑content and
                                                                                                                                   begin‑construction standards. Document Investment Tax Credit (ITC) and Production Tax Credit (PTC)
Under a 2 percent AV‑growth case, bond capacity declines by approximately $70 million; at a 6 percent interest‑rate
                                                                                                                                   assumptions in the cash‑flow model and include conservative haircuts where domestic content is not yet fully
case, capacity declines by approximately $40 million. Both outcomes are manageable within the district’s broader fiscal
                                                                                                                                   demonstrable.¹³
posture. Contingencies include pacing issuances across series, deferring lower‑priority amenities, and increasing the
                                                                                                                               ●   Contingency and escalation. Maintain program contingency and escalation allowances that reflect market
share of scopes that qualify for state participation or energy incentives.
                                                                                                                                   conditions. Update quarterly as bid results and commodity trends become available.

Question: Will Liberal Arts remain at it’s current location (Davies Hall)?                       Or swap locations with Science?


Conclusion: Liberal Arts will remain at it’s current location (Former Davies Hall).
A modernization by way of a new building will be designed and constructed.

Question: Can the Student Services be split in two parts?                                                      ...or consolidated and expand Tech. Ed with on-site diesel service?


Conclusion: Student Services is to remain as one consolidated program but can
be re-located as a new building at Rosa Parks Plaza. The Tech. Ed diesel service
will remain off-site but a new building will be planned at the suggested location
as part of the 25 year master plan.

of data gathered during the Discovery Phase with ARC. The goal of

deeper understanding of what is happening at ARC today. The
