ARC | Space Utilization Study




Classroom Usage
Patterns, Constraints + Opportunities
                                                                                               ARC Success Rates by Modality Spring 2024

This section explores classroom usage patterns, evolving modalities, ARC Executive
Team perspective on the longer-term modality mix, Student success rates by modality
and three scenarios based on varying levels of scheduling targets and Student demand.
The data that underlies the analysis presented here is derived from a number of
sources, which include:

•   Census reports for Fall 2018, Fall 2019, Fall 2022, Fall 2023 and Spring 2024
•   Classroom scheduling data for Fall 2019 and Spring 2024
•   ARC Leader workshop results from long term modality exercise
•   ARC modality success report

The opportunities indicated by analysis of the data in this section and the associated
three classroom scenarios could be significant for repurposed or reduced space.
However, there are a number of potential realities, which will need to be considered
before the full impact can be determined. These include but are not limited to:

•   Constancy of Student interest in the current modality mix
•   Appropriateness of encouraging Students in lower success categories to emphasize
    on-ground classes
•   Operational implications of shifting some instruction to other than Monday – Friday
    or to Non-Peak times
•   Willingness and appropriateness of Faculty to teach other than Monday – Thursday
    and in the afternoon / evening
•   Timing and transportation constraints of Adjunct Faculty who teach on multiple
    campuses
•   Ability of support capabilities to clean, service and maintain facilities and technology




                                                                                                                  Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 156
ARC | Space Utilization Study

Success Rates by Modality




This slide documents Student success statistics by modality for various Student groups. The consulting
team are not in a position to comment on the statistical significance of these numbers; however, it does
appear that:

•   In all cases online asynchronous success rates are below in-person success rates
•   Online synchronous has equal or higher success rates than in-person in 2 of the 3 groups
•   In all but one case online synchronous has better success rates than online asynchronous
•   African American and Hispanic Latino students have lower success rates than the overall average all
    modalities

ARC | Space Utilization Study




Hybrid Approach
Hybrid, Worker Profiles
and Work Modes
Traditionally, workplaces have been planned so that each person is
assigned a personal workspace, reflecting a 1:1 person to seat ratio.    Alone                  Working by yourself doing tasks that don’t require significant focus and/or
In a hybrid workplace for many employees work can occur at home,         Routine Tasks          privacy including email or casual correspondence.
in the office and other places. For some of these team members,
individual workspaces in the office are unassigned, and when in the
office these people select work settings that match their current mode   Alone                  Working by yourself doing tasks that require significant focus and/or privacy as
of work and their personal preference.                                                          in creating content, building spreadsheets or reading documents.
                                                                         Deep Focus Work
The key underlying factor for most effective hybrid workplace
strategies is the definition of worker profiles and types. These are
based on how individuals work and their level of mobility/choice today   Collaborate            Working with at least one other person and sharing information which could be
and in the future. Other factors that should be considered when          Sharing information    a typical meeting to update people or reviewing project progress.
developing a hybrid strategy are:
                                                                                                Working with at least one other person and creating content, idea sharing,
•   Cultural strengths and weakness of the organization                  Collaborate            brainstorming or innovation as in a product development meeting, or a problem-
•   Job function requirements                                            Creating content       solving session.
•   Current and desired degree of choice
•   Personal suitability or situation                                    Socialize              Spending time with others in a relaxed setting as in planned or chance
•   Resources to train and develop the hybrid worker                     Building connections   encounters, team bonding exercises, or celebrations.
•   Availability of mobile technology and infrastructure

                                                                                                This mode captures activities such as taking personal time, exercising, taking a
The profiles developed for this engagement are based on a deep           Other                  mental break, lunch, etc. that occur throughout the workday.
understanding of the time Classified Professionals spend in a range
of work modes. The work modes employed, and their definition were
first developed by workplace researchers Nonaka and Takeuchi.
Steelcase’s Workspace Futures team have expanded the knowledge
associated with the concept of work modes and we have leveraged
that information in this engagement.

ARC Work Mode Study




The work mode effort for this engagement involved one execution of the Applied Research +            The graph on this page documents the aggregate flow of work over a typical day at ARC across all
Consulting team’s Work Mode Study for 281 Classified Professionals and Classified Supervisors        team members and locations. For a specific individual, the flow and blend of activities varies
at ARC. 61 responses were received which represents a response rate of 21.7%. This response          depending on job role, department, and level. Personal work style and preferences will also impact
rate is lower than is typical and while the results for the broader population are usable, some of   the blend of work modes for a given person
the subcategories (locations beyond the main campus and Classified Supervisors) have
insufficient information and were excluded from reporting.                                           Please note, all work modes are important for an individual to be successful in their job and in
                                                                                                     general one work mode should not be unduly emphasized over another when considering the
                                                                                                     design of the workplace.




                                                                                                                                                   Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 179
ARC | Space Utilization Study




Collaborative Meeting Sizes

The work mode capability collects information from each collaborative
activity including the number of people in each session. This chart
documents the size of meetings for both collaborative work modes.
At ARC, in general meetings tend to be small.

•   The most frequent meeting size is 2 – 3 participants

•   The second most frequent meeting size is 4 - 6 participants

•   Approx. 79.7% of collaborative creating sessions include 2 to 6
    participants

•   Approx. 77.7% of collaborative sharing sessions include 2 to 6
    participants

Note in calculating percentages above “No amount specified” was
removed from the total.




                                                                        Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 180
ARC | Space Utilization Study




Work Mode
Aggregate Profile
                                This chart indicates the average percentage of time respondents
                                spend in each work mode (data here is aggregated across all
                                departments, locations and levels). Items of note at the aggregate
                                level are:

                                •   The predominant work mode is alone deep focus

                                •   64% of time is spent in alone work

                                •   The predominant collaborative activity is sharing

                                •   25% of time is spent in collaborative work

                                •   3% of time is spent in socializing

                                The following four pages break ARC’s work mode results into 8
                                unique profiles. This is sufficiently detailed to see unique aspects of
                                how work is done without introducing undue and unwarranted
                                complexity.

                                It should be noted that the various subdivisions (department, level
                                and location) analyzed may or may not have all profiles and the
                                percentage of time in each work mode will vary based on the unique
                                work patterns associated with a given profile in a specific subdivision.

Work Mode Profiles
By Department

The graphic on this page overviews the distribution of profiles by
department. The blue cells indicate the predominant profile by
department. Profile 3 is predominant for Administrative Services and
Profile 4 is predominant for Instructional Services and Student
Services and Support Programs.

It should be noted that each department has a range of profiles which
represent a diversity of job roles and personal preferences for how to
do a specific job.




                                                                         Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 186
ARC | Space Utilization Study




Work Mode Profiles
By Level

The graphics on this slide illustrate the distribution of work profiles for
Classified Professionals. The blue cells indicate the predominant
profile by level. As job levels increase within the organization the
distribution of profiles shift to the right (higher number profiles). In
essence this means the higher the level within the organization the
greater the tendency to spend time in collaborative activities.

For ARC the response rate was insufficient to provide data for
Classified Supervisors which means the distribution shift in profiles by
job level is not available here.

•   75.9% of Classified Professional are in profiles 1-4

ARC | Space Utilization Study




Work Mode Profiles
By Location

The graph and table on this slide illustrate the distribution of work
profiles by location. The blue cells indicate the predominant profile by

Note: the response rate was insufficient to provide data for Natomas
Center and Sacramento Regional Public Safety locations.




                                                                           Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 188
ARC | Space Utilization Study




Work Effectiveness
By Department

The tables on this page are based on aggregating data by
department across all work mode responses to the question “where
would you be most effective” office or home?

The data shows that in most instances team members believe from
an effectiveness / productivity perspective there is less reason for
alone work to be done in the office as compared to collaborative work
and socialization. Data from Administrative Services appears to vary
from the other two groups with a higher percentage of alone deep
focus in the office and a lower percentage of collaborative sharing in
the office. This is not typical and is likely due to low response rates for
this Study.

For the bar charts on this page, the numbers at the top of each bar
represent the days per week by department the average person
believes would be most effective to spend in the office. These are
derived by weighting headcount “effectiveness” responses by work
mode across each profile for each department.

The results from Instructional Services and Student Services and
Support are similar. Administrative Services data indicates a higher
need to be in the office.

Given the manner work modes overlap during a typical day, it would
probably be better to view these “days per week in the office” as
“hours per week in the office”.

                                06. Appendix



                                Observation
                                Key Findings




                                   Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 192
ARC | Space Utilization Study




Observation Overview
Intent + Overview

This section documents the results from Observation Study
conducted by the Applied Research + Consulting team during the
Spring semester of 2024 at ARC Main Campus and Natomas
Campus.

The intent of this effort was to gain a firsthand understanding of the
current state of spaces where learning and work happens, better
understand how space is used and the relationship of spaces to one
other. The observation effort included 17 Buildings/Portables including
approximately 45 Classrooms, 6 Faculty Office areas, 7 Classified
Professional work areas and the Natomas Center.

The following pages contain a summary of general observations as
well as a summary of learnings for each space type observed:

•   Classrooms
•   Faculty work areas
•   Classified Professional work areas
•   Student spaces
•   Natomas Center




                                                                          Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 193

                                Key Findings




                                   Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 204
ARC | Space Utilization Study




Workshops Overview

Overview
Intent + Overview


This section documents the results from workshops conducted with key stakeholder
groups at American River College by the Applied Research + Consulting team during the
Spring semester of 2024. A total of seven workshops were held and included two
sessions with Students, two sessions with Classified Professionals and three sessions
with Faculty, including one session with Faculty who previously were located in Davies
Hall.

These workshops were intended to further engage Students, Faculty and Classified
Professionals in the discovery process, to better understand their perspective on the
current experience at ARC, and to explore what would be valued in the future learning
and work experience.

The following pages reflect a summary of the workshops including key findings, direct
quotes from participants and photos from the sessions.

group work, create equitable experiences and encourage more               Students lack awareness of how to effectively access information.       can connect and support one another. Students commented there is
interaction with Faculty. Students expressed feelings of presence         Barriers such as which side of campus you park on and walk from,        confusion around navigating available services and finding out how to
disparity for online/asynchronous classes.                                who you know, and social media presence affect how they learn           access them, comparing it to a labyrinth and a treasure hunt.

Infrastructure                                                            HomeBases                                                               Wellbeing

contamination (resulting in allergic reactions), as well as a lack of     build community, while others liked having them near their department   many ideas shared by Students to help address wellness on campus.

                                                                                                                                                         Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 207
ARC | Space Utilization Study




Student Workshops
Collaging Exercise

Students workshop participants were divided into groups and asked
to collectively build a collage using a selection of images. The
collages served as a process by which Students could ideate, explore
and share perspectives regarding the ideal future campus
experience.

The following questions were considered:

     • What will help you be successful?
     • What will inspire you?
     • Where is the heart of the campus?
     • What will make it feel like a community?
     • How will you connect to Faculty?
     • How will you connect with other students?
     • What services/amenities are important to you?

Students were highly engaged and provided robust and thoughtful
feedback. A summary of the feedback and key themes from the            Above are images of Student groups creating collages during the workshops.
Student workshops are provided in this section of the report.

Classified Professionals
Key Findings
Common themes emerged from Classified Professionals regarding their current and ideal future work experience at American River College.



Meaningful Connections and Interactions                                   Effective Communication                                                 Student-Centric Approach
Workshop participants value connections with their colleagues and         Workshop participants expressed the need for more transparent           There is a desire for an environment where every Student thrives but
interactions with Students. They hope for more opportunities to work      communication in the future. They highlighted several areas of          there is a concern that the pre-pandemic approach will not
with other departments and to connect informally with colleagues.         potential improvement to increase transparency and awareness            adequately support the demands of a new generation of Students.
They recognize the importance of maintaining strong relationships         among employees and across departments e.g., the high volume of         They hope that class schedules will prioritize Student demand for
both in person and online to support Student demand.                      emails leads to missed communications; the current website is           mixed modalities and ensure equitable considerations for
                                                                          difficult to navigate for both Employees and Students.                  disadvantaged students when deciding between online or in-person
                                                                                                                                                  education.
Impact of Hybrid Work on Student Services                                 Process Improvement                                                     Support and Resource Enhancement
Hybrid Work has been embraced and participants believe it has             Workshop participants commented that many processes have been           Classified Professionals feel that recent employee turnover has
positively impacted how they support Students. Shifts in demand for in-   updated to support the shift to online working and learning. However,   negatively impacted some services and departments. They
person and/or online support can be responded to more flexibly. They      they hope for more a proactive response to changes required for the     emphasized the need for additional staffing and resources,
believe demand for in-person support will increase as more Students       future rather than a reactive response, especially in the areas of      particularly in IT positions and Student Services e.g., increased
return on-ground but expect online support will still be required.        onboarding, training and professional development.                      demand for Interpreters / Translators and Counsellors.


Infrastructure Maintenance                                                Promote Work-Life Balance + Wellbeing
There is a sense of frustration and belief that the current               Classified Professionals value work-life balance and flexible
infrastructure is not keeping up with the changes in work process         schedules. They hope for an equitable hybrid program and continued
e.g., delays in infrastructure improvements and addressing safety         autonomy over time and presence in the office.
concerns across the Campus.

ARC | Space Utilization Study




Classified Professionals Workshops
Exercise 1
Values Framework
The exercise is intended to capture elements of the work experience at American River College that Classified Professionals treasure, want to trash, hope for, and fear in the future.

                                                                                                       Value
                      TREASURES                                                                            HOPES
                           •    Hybrid work blending on ground and online                                      • Effective communication, leadership collaboration, and clear direction
                           •    Community built through social engagement, teamwork, and effective             • Supportive work environment with opportunities for growth, consistency, and flexibility
                                communication                                                                  • Work-life balance and flexible schedules, including campus closure on Fridays
                           •    Meaningful connections among students and colleagues                           • Website clarity and accessibility for easier navigation
                           •    Balance of in-person and online Student interactions                           • Support for language diversity and Student accessibility
                           •    Dedicated, individual workspaces                                               • Additional staffing and resources, including increased IT and Student services staffing
                           •    Autonomy over time and presence                                                • Expedited hiring process and comprehensive technology training for new staff
                           •    Work-life balance
                           •    Ability to rejuvenate

            Have                                                                                                                                                                                Don’t Have
                      TRASH                                                                                FEARS
                           •    Complex hiring and acquisition processes                                       • Insufficient communication, especially with rapid organizational changes
                           •    Deficient onboarding and training processes for new hires                      • Scheduling practices which prioritize Faculty convenience over Student needs
                           •    Challenges managing organizational changes effectively, including              • Lack of consideration for disadvantaged students' needs when deciding online vs. on ground courses
                                management transitions and program implementations                             • Inadequate training for support workers to adapt to hybrid work models
                           •    Deficient communication, including late responses + anonymous emails           • Ignore challenges of shared offices, including personal preferences
                           •    Hostility and disregard for staff expertise and feedback                       • Disregard for necessary changes, including infrastructure improvements and increased resources
                           •    Lack of transparency and resistance to change                                  • Failure to promptly address safety concerns and building maintenance issues
                           •    Inadequate workspace for students and inequitable office setups                • Inflexibility and lack of work-life balance, which can lead to burnout and bad morale
                           •    Inflexible work schedules and micromanagement of productivity
                           •    Insufficient support, resources, spaces and amenities
                                                                                                 Don’t Value

Faculty Workshops
Key Findings
Common themes emerged from Faculty regarding their current and ideal future experience at ARC.




Focus on Student Success                                                 Importance of Connection                                                     Lack of Trust
Faculty highly value their relationships with Students with a            With the closing of Davies Hall, the importance of connection and            Faculty described decision making at ARC as “top-down” and believe
preference for in-person interactions. They expressed gratitude for      community has been emphasized. Faculty value collaborating with              there is a lack of transparency. They shared instances of territoriality
the many services offered to Students as well as the Classified          colleagues and Students and appreciate having spaces in which to             and feel they are often pitted against one another for resources. The
Professionals that support them (e.g. HomeBase Staff).                   meet and mentor. Proximity and access are important to Faculty.              Davies Hall experience appears to have negatively impacted trust
                                                                                                                                                      among a number of Faculty members.


Improving Equity + Inclusion                                             Creating Successful Online Experiences                                       Fear of Temporary becoming Permanent
Faculty are looking to offer greater access to programs and events by    With the increase in online instruction, additional time is often required   There is a palpable fear from Faculty that the temporary Davies
providing additional interpretive services (e.g. ASL) so more Students   to prepare and Faculty fear work creep. They shared examples of              replacement strategy will become permanent. Faculty is concerned
have the opportunity to participate. Faculty believe departments and     having to “chase enrollment” to ensure an in-person class and                the look and feel of the portables does not convey an appropriate
groups are siloed and acknowledge there is a benefit to all for more     expressed frustration over online cheating. Faculty would value working      collegiate experience for existing and prospective Students and Staff.
sharing of best practices across disciplines.                            with an Online Curriculum Coordinator or similar expert resource to
                                                                         provide training in digital course design and teaching .

Infrastructure                                                           Processes + Enhancing Efficiencies                                           Resources + Amenities
Faculty expressed concerns about facilities and infrastructures. They    Faculty expressed concerns over Students struggling to find                  Faculty voiced concerns over a lack of faith/prayer spaces as well as
desire more modern, flexible spaces for learning environments and        appropriate resources and discussed ways to simplify processes for           food options for the diverse religious populations. Faculty wish to
events, and the tools, technology and equipment to support both on       Students. Faculty feel the campus is hard to navigate and believe            ensure there remains space for Student clubs and groups to meet on
ground and online modalities. Faculty also expressed a desire for        new Students might benefit from an enhanced on-boarding process.             campus. Faculty would like to see food service and resources
better mass transit access.                                                                                                                           available on campus in the evenings.

Davies Hall Workshop
Exercise 2 | Values Framework

The exercise is intended to capture elements of the ARC learning and work experience that Faculty treasure, want to trash, hope for, and fear in the future.



                                                                                                 Value
                        TREASURES                                                                        HOPES
                                • Students have quick and easy access to professors                          • Proximity to Students, resources, classrooms, and colleagues
                                • Access to resources to be able to guide Students quickly                   • Good ventilation
                                • Space to focus                                                             • Access to a covered area similar to Howard Hall where Students
                                                                                                               can sit, work together or do homework before and after class
                                                                                                             • Focus spaces for Faculty
                                                                                                             • Ability to quickly access other colleagues
                                                                                                             • A space that allows for innovation and inspiration for both Students
                                                                                                               and Faculty

            Have                                                                                                                                                                            Don’t Have
                        TRASH                                                                            FEARS
                                •   Displaced class locations – being spread out over campus                 •   Students will not find ARC as a destination campus anymore
                                •   Time spent moving supplies across campus                                 •   Students will be in portables that are not up to the standard of
                                •   Overcrowded classrooms in portables                                          other colleges
                                •   Environments not conducive to group work                                 •   Moving to online classes as a solution to solve the space issue
                                •   Noise transfer                                                           •   No one asking “What should a classroom look like“
                                                                                                             •   Promises that are not kept, voices not being heard, and being paid
                                                                                                                 lip service

                                                                                                                 for not replacing Davies Hall classrooms and offices

                                06. Appendix



                                Space Utilization
                                Survey
                                Key Findings




                                   Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 226
ARC | Space Utilization Study




Overview
Introduction

This section contains an overview of Key Findings from the Survey conducted for
Students, Faculty, and Classified Professionals from March 28th to April 22nd 2024. The
survey measures perspectives about the current experiences and patterns on
campus, online, and in classrooms.

Sampling was not employed, and the response levels were:


      62.1% for Faculty
      (215 of 346 Faculty responded)

      86.5% for Classified Professionals
      (211 of 244 Classified Professionals responded)

The invitation was sent to all Students, and there were 117 responses, representing a
low response rate; however, sufficient responses were received for the data to be
usable.

A detailed report documenting the Experience Survey results is in a separate PDF titled
“Space Utilization Study: Experience Survey Report.”

               of the top 5 reasons to come to the campus are to be

Faculty
Individual Work Experience




    38%                         of Faculty time is spent working Alone,
                                either on Routine Tasks or Deep Focus




   63% of respondents indicate that the workplace does not
   “completely” support Alone: Deep Focus work.

   87% of respondents believe the workplace does not completely
   support Rejuvenation.

   83% of respondents indicate they are not “completely satisfied”
   with the “choice of different places” when working alone.




                                                                          Steelcase | Applied Research + Consulting | Confidential | 240
ARC | Space Utilization Study




Faculty
Teaching Experience




    37%                         of Faculty time is spent Teaching




   54% of respondents indicate the workplace does not “completely
   support” Teaching.

   61% of respondents indicate they do not “completely agree” that
   “classrooms help to facilitate group learning between students”.

   65% of respondents indicate they do not “completely agree” that
   “classrooms support their preferred pedagogy.” It is the lowest
   rating of the four categories.

Faculty
Teaching Experience




    89%
                                of respondents do not “completely agree”
                                that classrooms support a blend of in
                                person and online learning




   A significant percentage of Faculty conduct a blend of online and
   on ground courses.

   support the ability to “see” or “hear” content.

ARC | Space Utilization Study




Faculty
Collaborative Experience




    15%                         of Faculty time is spent Collaborating,
                                either Sharing or Creating




   Approximately two-thirds of respondents indicate the workplace
   does not “completely support” Collaboration, either Sharing or
   Creating.

   Faculty spend 3% of their time in Socialization, however 80% of
   respondents believe the workplace does not support it.

   84+% of respondents indicate they are not “completely satisfied”
   with access to “visually private” and “acoustically private”
   group spaces or “collaborative spaces for unscheduled
   meetings.”
